topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a low-latency fusion framework, RealTimeFold, that dynamically integrates heterogeneous experimental data streams (e.g., Cryo-EM, SAXS, mass spectrometry) with evolving sequence information to incrementally update protein structure predictions in real time during clinical diagnostics.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction systems, including AlphaFold2 and its extensions, operate offline and require complete datasets before delivering predictions, thereby limiting their clinical utility where new experimental data is generated continuously and decisions must be made rapidly. The open problem is the absence of algorithms capable of fusing multi-modal, streaming biochemical and biophysical data with sequence inputs under strict latency constraints, while maintaining or exceeding AlphaFold-level accuracy. This gap restricts timely protein structure-based clinical decisions in contexts such as precision medicine and rapid pathogen characterization.

APPROACH:  
RealTimeFold introduces a modular, incremental prediction architecture combining a novel streaming transformer encoder with a graph neural network optimized for partial and heterogeneous input updates. It fuses real-time Cryo-EM density maps (processed via a lightweight deep-tracer module), small-angle X-ray scattering profiles, and mass spectrometry cross-linking data alongside sequence embeddings derived from evolutionary data updated on the fly. The system employs a latency-aware attention mechanism and adaptive confidence recalibration to prioritize recent data without retraining from scratch, enabling continuous refinement of the protein structural model within seconds after new data arrives.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate RealTimeFold on benchmark datasets including Cryo-EM Model Challenge 2023, SAXS BDB (BioSAXS Data Bank), and PRIDE mass spectrometry datasets, using baselines AlphaFold2, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and RoseTTAFold. Metrics include TM-score improvement ≥3% over baselines on incomplete/streaming inputs, end-to-end update latency under 5 seconds per new data packet (vs. >30 minutes offline for baselines), and robustness measured by continuous model accuracy over incremental data updates. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs, with five random seeds and ablations to isolate impact of each data modality and latency-aware components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work focused on offline high-accuracy prediction (AlphaFold2) or low-resolution map enhancement (DeepTracer-LowResEnhance), RealTimeFold is the first system designed to support real-time incremental fusion of heterogeneous experimental and sequence data with strict latency constraints, enabling adaptive protein structure prediction during ongoing clinical workflows rather than post hoc analysis.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a probabilistic deep generative framework integrating physics-informed neural networks and sparse experimental constraints to model full conformational ensembles of intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs), explicitly capturing their structural heterogeneity and dynamic flexibility beyond single-structure predictions like AlphaFold.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art protein structure predictors, including AlphaFold2, excel at determining static, folded protein conformations but fail to represent the inherent conformational heterogeneity and dynamic ensembles of IDPs. This deficiency limits understanding of IDP functions and impedes drug discovery targeting these proteins. The open problem is to accurately generate and validate probabilistic conformational ensembles that reflect the true structural diversity of IDPs under physiological conditions, using sparse and noisy experimental data such as NMR and SAXS, which deterministic models cannot adequately incorporate or quantify uncertainty over.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid generative model combining variational autoencoders (VAEs) with physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to learn continuous latent representations of IDP ensemble conformations constrained by experimental observables (e.g., chemical shifts, NOEs, SAXS profiles). The model integrates differentiable molecular force fields as soft priors to enforce physical realism, enabling sampling of diverse plausible conformers with associated uncertainty estimates. We incorporate Bayesian model calibration to fuse sparse experimental data, thereby refining ensemble distributions and quantifying confidence intervals for structural features. This approach explicitly models plasticity and dynamic disorder rather than collapsing to a single consensus structure.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on established IDP benchmarks including the DisProt database (containing experimentally characterized IDPs), the Protein Ensemble Database (PED), and the NMR Chemical Shift Database (BMRB). Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepFragLib, and flexible ensemble modeling approaches such as Flexible-Meccano and ENSEMBLE. Quantitative metrics will comprise ensemble agreement scores (e.g., ensemble RMSD vs experimental restraints), goodness-of-fit to SAXS profiles (Chi-squared, R-factor), chemical shift RMSD reduction by >10%, and uncertainty calibration metrics (expected calibration error <5%). Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a computational budget of 2 weeks per dataset, running 5 independent seeds and ablation studies on PINN loss terms and Bayesian calibration components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike AlphaFold2 and DeepFragLib, which produce static or limited structural predictions, this method explicitly models IDP conformational heterogeneity as probabilistic ensembles constrained by sparse experimental data, incorporating physics priors within deep generative frameworks. It advances beyond prior deterministic or heuristic ensemble methods by providing calibrated uncertainty quantification and end-to-end integration of experimental constraints, enabling mechanistic insights and drug design targeting IDP structural plasticity.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a deep generative framework, ConformaGAN, that produces diverse, biochemically constrained conformational ensembles of proteins from sequence and static AlphaFold structures, explicitly modeling ligand-induced allosteric states to enhance dynamic drug binding site prediction.

BACKGROUND:  
Current AlphaFold-based methods primarily predict single, static protein conformations, lacking the ability to capture the full spectrum of biologically relevant dynamic states crucial for drug discovery, particularly ligand-induced and allosteric conformers. Existing conformational ensemble generation approaches either rely on computationally expensive molecular dynamics or generate ensembles without explicit biochemical constraints, limiting their utility for actionable drug design hypotheses.

APPROACH:  
ConformaGAN leverages a conditional generative adversarial network architecture that integrates (1) static AlphaFold-predicted structures as structural priors, (2) biochemical constraints including residue flexibility profiles and experimentally derived hydrogen-deuterium exchange data, and (3) ligand-binding site-aware conditioning vectors to generate ensembles of protein conformations reflecting functionally relevant dynamics. Training employs joint optimization of adversarial and reconstruction losses with a novel biochemical consistency regularizer that enforces physically plausible backbone and side-chain variations. The model outputs interpretable ensembles representing multiple allosteric and ligand-induced states, enabling downstream drug binding site refinement and virtual screening.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) the Protein Data Bank’s ensemble subset for proteins with multiple solved conformations, (2) the DynBench dataset of protein dynamics with annotated ligand-induced conformational changes, and (3) the Binding MOAD database for ligand-bound states. Baselines include AlphaFold2 static predictions, MD-based ensemble generation (e.g., AMBER MD simulations), and recent deep learning ensemble methods like DeepEnsemble and ConformerNet. Metrics include structural diversity quantified by ensemble RMSD range, ligand docking enrichment factors (EF1% and EF5%), and biochemical plausibility scores (Ramachandran statistics and hydrogen bond preservation), targeting >5% absolute improvement in docking EF1% over static AlphaFold structures and >10% reduction in computational cost vs MD baselines. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with three independent training seeds; ablation studies will isolate the impact of biochemical constraints and ligand-aware conditioning.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative ensemble methods that mainly produce unconstrained or purely MD-based dynamic samples, ConformaGAN explicitly incorporates biochemical constraints and ligand-binding context, enabling generation of interpretable, functionally relevant conformational variants that AlphaFold and existing ensemble models cannot produce. This approach directly addresses the critical gap of modeling ligand-induced allostery in a computationally efficient, data-driven manner, significantly advancing structure-based drug discovery pipelines beyond static predictions.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a generative deep learning framework that explicitly models and samples rare, low-probability conformational states of disease-related proteins, leveraging physics-informed variational autoencoders and enhanced conformational energy landscapes to predict functionally critical but underrepresented protein structures beyond AlphaFold’s dominant fold predictions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods like AlphaFold accurately predict the most stable, dominant conformations of proteins but fail to capture rare conformational states that are often transient, low-probability, and critical for understanding disease mechanisms and drug binding. These rare states are severely underrepresented in existing structural databases such as PDB and training datasets, causing models to lack generalization for functionally relevant alternative folds. There is an unmet need for computational methods that can extrapolate beyond common folds to identify these elusive states with physical plausibility and biological relevance.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel generative model combining a physics-informed variational autoencoder (VAE) trained on multiple conformational ensembles from datasets like DE Shaw’s long-timescale molecular dynamics trajectories and structural ensembles from NMR data (e.g., Protein Ensemble Database, PED). The model integrates learned latent representations with energy-based constraints derived from coarse-grained molecular mechanics potentials to enforce physical plausibility during sampling. This framework will be conditioned on disease-related protein families (e.g., amyloid-beta, p53 mutants) to synthesize rare conformations with experimentally validated functional relevance. AlphaFold2 and RoseTTAFold will serve as baseline dominant-state predictors, while enhanced sampling MD methods (e.g., Weighted Ensemble, Metadynamics) provide baselines for rare state generation comparison.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including Protein Ensemble Database (PED), DE Shaw MD simulation datasets (long-timescale folding trajectories), and the Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP14) rare conformer targets. Metrics include recovery rate of experimentally validated rare states measured by RMSD ≤ 2.5 Å to known alternative conformations, coverage of conformational diversity quantified by ensemble entropy increase ≥ 15% over baselines, and improvement in binding pose prediction accuracy (>5% absolute increase in docking success rate) for disease-relevant ligands. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~1000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds for statistical robustness and ablation studies to isolate the impact of physics-based regularization and latent space conditioning.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike AlphaFold2 and RoseTTAFold, which focus on predicting the single dominant fold and fail to extrapolate to rare states, and unlike enhanced sampling MD methods which are computationally expensive and limited in scalability, this approach uses a generative, physics-informed latent space model that explicitly targets low-probability, functionally critical conformations for disease-related proteins. This provides a scalable and physically plausible method to discover novel conformations inaccessible to current SOTA predictors or standard MD techniques.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a novel end-to-end, multi-stage protein structure prediction pipeline that compresses and quantizes AlphaFold-inspired models with adaptive accuracy-latency tradeoffs, enabling sub-500ms inference on ARM-based edge devices while maintaining TM-score degradation below 5%, validated on CASP14, CAMEO, and ProteinNet datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
The core open problem is that current state-of-the-art protein folding models like AlphaFold2 deliver highly accurate predictions but require extensive computational resources and cloud infrastructure, making them unsuitable for real-time or near-real-time deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing model compression and quantization approaches either degrade accuracy beyond acceptable limits or fail to meet latency constraints under 500ms, limiting practical applications in clinical environments or fieldwork where immediate prediction feedback is critical.

APPROACH:  
We propose a latency-optimized, hierarchical compression framework combining structured pruning, mixed-precision quantization, and knowledge distillation tailored for AlphaFold-inspired architectures. This pipeline introduces an adaptive inference mode that dynamically adjusts model complexity based on input sequence characteristics and hardware resource availability to optimize latency versus accuracy tradeoffs. Additionally, we integrate a lightweight, edge-friendly folding refinement module that corrects coarse predictions post-quantization to recover lost structural detail without significant runtime increase.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on CASP14, CAMEO, and ProteinNet benchmark datasets, comparing against AlphaFold2, OpenFold, and FastFold baselines. Metrics include TM-score (target within 5% of full-precision AlphaFold2), RMSD, and inference latency measured on representative ARM-based edge devices (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano, Raspberry Pi 4). Experiments will include ablation studies on pruning ratios, quantization bit-widths, and adaptive mode thresholds, with at least five random seeds per configuration to ensure statistical robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior edge-focused inference methods that apply static compression or focus solely on model size reduction, our approach uniquely incorporates adaptive complexity scaling combined with a dedicated lightweight refinement step to maintain high prediction fidelity under strict latency constraints. This enables accurate, sub-second protein folding predictions on widely accessible ARM-based hardware, which existing methods such as FastFold or DeepTracer-LowResEnhance do not achieve.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified scaling law model for distributed large language model training that incorporates heterogeneous cloud hardware variability, dynamic network conditions, and adaptive communication compression techniques to predict training time and efficiency with under 5% error while enabling optimized resource allocation.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling law models for LLM training, including the winning “Communication-Aware Distributed Scaling Laws for Batch Cloud Training,” assume relatively stable network and hardware conditions and focus mainly on fixed communication overheads and synchronization delays. However, real-world cloud environments are heterogeneous and dynamic, with variable network latency, bandwidth fluctuations, and differing hardware capabilities, which cause nonlinear and unpredictable slowdowns that existing models do not account for. This results in suboptimal training schedules and resource usage under practical deployment. The open problem is to build a predictive scaling law framework that explicitly models and adapts to these real-world variabilities to improve predictive accuracy and efficiency optimization.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-factor distributed scaling law framework that integrates (1) a detailed statistical model of hardware heterogeneity (e.g., GPU/TPU generation differences, memory bandwidth), (2) stochastic models of network latency and bandwidth variability based on real cloud telemetry, and (3) adaptive communication compression techniques (like quantization or sparsification) with their trade-offs on accuracy and latency. The model uses an augmented system of nonlinear equations combining compute, communication, and compression costs, calibrated from large-scale synthetic benchmarks and real cloud training logs (e.g., GPT-2, GPT-3) collected on diverse heterogeneous clusters. This allows precise prediction of wall-clock training time and scaling efficiency across varying batch sizes and cluster configurations. The framework also outputs resource allocation recommendations to minimize total cost while meeting target training timelines.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the GPT-2 and GPT-3 pretraining workloads using logs from Microsoft Azure, AWS, and Google Cloud heterogeneous clusters. Validate prediction accuracy of wall-clock time and scaling efficiency with expected errors below 5%. Compare against baselines including Communication-Aware Distributed Scaling Laws (prior SOTA), idealized scaling laws assuming homogeneous hardware, and naive linear scaling models. Metrics will include prediction error (%), training throughput (tokens/sec), and resource utilization efficiency. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of hardware heterogeneity modeling and communication compression adaptation. Experiments will run on clusters with 64 to 256 GPUs/TPUs, using fixed 10% training compute budget relative to standard pretraining runs, with 3 seeds per configuration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, which assumes stable and homogeneous hardware/network conditions, our framework explicitly models the complex real-world heterogeneity and temporal variability in cloud training environments. It uniquely integrates adaptive communication compression impacts into scaling laws, enabling both more accurate prediction and actionable resource allocation strategies. This capability to model and optimize under real cloud dynamics is not addressed by existing scaling laws or inverse problem approaches.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop a data-centric adaptive scaling law framework that dynamically allocates compute resources during cloud-scale LLM pretraining by modeling data distribution drift and hardware heterogeneity to optimize both training efficiency and downstream task performance.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for large language models assume static, uniform data distributions and homogeneous compute environments, neglecting the realities of cloud-scale pretraining pipelines where data continuously evolves and hardware differs in capabilities. This mismatch leads to suboptimal compute allocation, causing training inefficiencies and degraded downstream generalization, especially when data distribution shifts or resource heterogeneity arises at scale.

APPROACH: We propose a novel adaptive scaling law framework that integrates real-time monitoring of data distribution shifts (via embedding-based drift detection) and resource heterogeneity profiles to dynamically adjust model size, batch size, and training iterations per data segment. This framework employs a multi-objective optimization algorithm that balances compute cost, data novelty, and hardware heterogeneity, enabling selective compute scaling and early stopping on drifting data subsets. The system continuously updates scaling parameters using a feedback loop from downstream validation metrics on key tasks.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on the C4 dataset and the OpenWebText2 corpus to simulate evolving web-scale data streams, combined with hardware heterogeneity simulated from GPU clusters comprising NVIDIA A100, RTX 3090, and TPU v3 pods. Baselines include GPT-3 (175B), PaLM, and the compute-optimal scaling laws from Kaplan et al. (2020). Metrics measured will include downstream perplexity on WikiText-103, zero-shot accuracy on SuperGLUE, and compute efficiency defined as training FLOPs per unit downstream metric improvement, targeting >5% perplexity reduction and 20% compute savings over baselines. Ablations will analyze the impact of drift detection sensitivity and resource heterogeneity modeling. Experiments will run on cloud TPU v4 pods with 4 random seeds to ensure statistical significance.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior scaling laws that treat data and compute resources as static and homogeneous, this method explicitly incorporates evolving data distribution and heterogeneous hardware into the scaling decision process, enabling dynamic, data-driven compute allocation that prior approaches cannot achieve. This closes the gap between theoretical scaling laws and practical cloud-scale pretraining pipelines, yielding superior training efficiency and downstream robustness in realistic environments.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive inference framework for large language models that integrates real-time energy consumption profiling with token-level confidence dynamics to detect phase transitions in model certainty, enabling early stopping and computation scaling to optimize accuracy and energy use under strict power constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current large language model (LLM) inference systems do not effectively integrate energy consumption metrics with probabilistic confidence dynamics, resulting in inefficient computation and missed opportunities for early detection of failure or uncertainty states. Existing scaling laws for LLMs primarily focus on training regimes or static performance scaling rather than dynamic, energy-aware inference strategies that can adapt computation depth in real-time. This gap limits deployment of LLMs on energy-constrained edge devices where power and latency budgets are critical.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel energy-confidence phase transition detection (EC-PTD) method that continuously monitors energy per token and the evolving confidence distribution of token predictions during LLM autoregressive decoding. By modeling confidence trajectories alongside fine-grained energy profiles, our method identifies early indicators of impending uncertainty spikes or failure modes—referred to as phase transitions—and dynamically adjusts inference depth (e.g., number of layers executed or precision scaling) to reduce wasted computation. EC-PTD uses a lightweight online classifier trained on combined energy-confidence feature embeddings to trigger adaptive intervention under a strict power budget, ensuring inference stays below a configurable wattage threshold without sacrificing output quality.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will validate EC-PTD on three benchmark datasets: the GLUE/SuperGLUE benchmark for natural language understanding tasks, SQuAD 2.0 for question answering, and Penn Treebank for language modeling. Baselines include GPT-3 (175B), GPT-4, and BERT-large in their standard full-inference modes. Evaluation metrics are energy per inference (targeting <5W average power on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX), confidence calibration error (expected reduction by at least 10%), and detection latency of failure states (goal: under 50ms post phase transition onset). Experiments will run on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX hardware with three random seeds per setup, including ablations on confidence features and energy granularity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that separately addresses scaling laws or confidence-based early stopping, EC-PTD uniquely fuses real-time energy consumption with token-level confidence dynamics to detect phase transitions in model uncertainty. This coupling enables proactive, adaptive computation scaling that no existing LLM scaling law or inference method currently provides, especially under stringent power and latency constraints on edge devices.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Data-Curated Scaling Laws for Low-Resource Languages via Adaptive Data Weighting and Subset Selection to Maximize Generalization under Severe Data Scarcity and Noise.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) assume abundant and relatively clean pretraining data, failing to adequately address the challenges posed by low-resource languages and dialects where data is scarce, noisy, and domain-specific. This leads to suboptimal scaling behavior, overfitting, and poor generalization. The open problem is how to formulate and empirically validate scaling laws that explicitly incorporate curated data selection and weighting strategies tailored to low-resource linguistic settings to improve learning efficiency and robustness.

APPROACH:  
We propose a principled framework that integrates adaptive data subset selection and weighting into the LLM pretraining pipeline, guided by metrics of data scarcity, noise level, and linguistic domain specificity. Using a multi-objective optimization scheme, the method dynamically prioritizes and reweights scarce but high-value data subsets during training. We derive empirical scaling laws that relate model size, effective data quantity (post-curation), and data quality scores to downstream performance, explicitly modeling the impact of curated training data on scaling trends. This approach incorporates iterative noise estimation and linguistic coverage metrics to refine the data curation process, enabling scalable training that generalizes well on low-resource languages.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on low-resource language benchmarks such as FLORES-200 (multilingual translation), Masakhane NLG (African languages generation), and the IndicCorp dataset (Indic languages corpus). Baselines include GPT-2 (small and medium variants), mBERT, and XLM-R. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in BLEU scores on FLORES-200, reduce perplexity by >15% on Masakhane NLG test sets, and improve F1 by >5 points on IndicCorp language modeling tasks. Experiments are run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 2 million token training budget per language, five random seeds per configuration, including ablations on weighting schemes and data subset sizes.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling law studies which assume monolithic clean datasets and do not model data scarcity or noise explicitly, this work concretely formulates and validates scaling laws that integrate adaptive data curation and weighting to handle severely limited and noisy low-resource language corpora. This enables principled training of LLMs that scale efficiently and generalize robustly in linguistically diverse and data-poor regimes—something no previous scaling law or LLM training methodology has achieved.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-inspired energy-based temporal scaling law model that explicitly characterizes phase transitions in large language models’ performance under real-time interactive latency and input distribution fluctuations, enabling accurate prediction and adaptive scaling strategies for responsive LLM deployments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs primarily focus on static metrics such as parameter count, training compute, or data volume, and do not capture the dynamic temporal behaviors critical in real-time interactive systems where user input patterns and latency constraints vary rapidly. This leads to unreliable performance predictions and suboptimal scaling decisions in applications like conversational agents or interactive assistants, where responsiveness and stability under fluctuating loads are paramount.

APPROACH:  
We propose an energy-based model inspired by statistical physics to represent LLM scaling dynamics as a function of time-varying input distributions and latency constraints, explicitly modeling phase transitions that indicate shifts in system behavior (e.g., from stable to overloaded regimes). This involves defining a temporal energy landscape over model states driven by user interaction dynamics and latency budgets, and fitting this model to large-scale interaction logs. The framework enables principled prediction of performance bottlenecks and guides adaptive resource scaling and scheduling policies to maintain responsiveness.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on multi-turn interactive benchmarks such as DSTC10 (Dialogue System Technology Challenge 10), MultiWOZ 2.4 (multi-domain task-oriented dialogue), and the LiveQA dataset for real-time question answering. Baselines include GPT-4, PaLM 2, and LLaMA 2 fine-tuned for interactive tasks. Metrics: reduce average response latency by ≥30% under peak user loads, achieve >2% absolute improvement in turn-level F1 score on MultiWOZ 2.4, and maintain perplexity below 20 on LiveQA during real-time streaming. Experiments will use a fixed compute budget (~1M GPU hours across NVIDIA A100s), with 5 random seeds per configuration and ablation studies isolating the effect of temporal phase transition modeling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws that treat performance as a static function of model size and data, this method captures temporal fluctuations and phase transitions specific to real-time user interaction dynamics and latency requirements, providing actionable insights for adaptive scaling—capabilities absent in existing inverse problem or static scaling analyses.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive, low-overhead quantum error correction framework for NISQ devices that integrates real-time noise characterization with machine learning-driven error model updates, enabling dynamic, data-efficient error correction tailored to fluctuating noise environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices suffer from excessive qubit overhead and static error models that fail to adapt to rapidly changing noise patterns intrinsic to near-term quantum hardware. Traditional QEC codes like the surface code require large physical qubit counts and cannot dynamically adjust to environmental fluctuations or device drift, limiting practical scalability and error resilience during extended operation. There is a critical need for QEC schemes that efficiently leverage limited calibration data to maintain robust error correction in the presence of time-varying noise without prohibitive resource costs.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid framework combining a low-overhead QEC code optimized for NISQ constraints (inspired by but improving upon the 10-qubit codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices”) with an adaptive machine learning module that continuously refines its noise model via online learning from sparse calibration data and in-situ syndrome measurements. This ML module uses reinforcement learning and Bayesian updating to predict and mitigate evolving noise channels on-the-fly, feeding updated error parameters back to dynamically adjust the QEC decoding strategy. The system operates within a tight resource budget, leveraging lightweight neural networks and efficient classical-quantum interfacing to minimize latency and overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on publicly available NISQ device noise datasets such as IBM’s Quantum Device Noise Dataset (Qiskit Pulse data), Rigetti’s Quantum Cloud Services noise profiles, and the Quantum Error Correction Benchmark Dataset (QECBench). Baselines include standard surface code implementations, the low-overhead QEC code from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices,” and static machine learning-based error mitigation methods such as “Adaptive Machine Learning for Error Mitigation in Near-Term Quantum Sensors.” Metrics: achieve >30% reduction in logical error rates compared to static QEC baselines, reduce qubit overhead by at least 50% relative to surface code, and maintain error correction performance within <5% degradation across time-varying noise intervals. Experimental protocol includes 5 independent runs with different noise profiles, ablation studies on ML model components, and hardware-in-the-loop tests on IBM Quantum devices with a compute budget of ~200 GPU hours for ML training and decoding simulations.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either use static QEC codes with fixed error models or require extensive calibration data for ML-based mitigation, this method uniquely combines adaptive online learning with a low-overhead QEC code to dynamically track and correct errors in real time under fluctuating noise conditions typical of NISQ devices, achieving both practical resource efficiency and sustained error resilience previously unattainable.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop an ultra-low overhead, adaptive noise-aware quantum error correction framework with real-time noise profiling optimized for quantum sensors on NISQ devices, enabling sub-microsecond latency error mitigation without qubit overhead explosion.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) approaches for NISQ devices, including low-overhead codes and optimized compilers, fail to meet the strict latency and overhead constraints required for real-time adaptive correction in near-term quantum sensors. Noise profiles in these sensors fluctuate dynamically, demanding immediate error correction responses that traditional QEC codes and noise characterization methods cannot provide without significant qubit or time overhead, limiting practical deployment.

APPROACH: We propose an integrated system combining (1) a fast, hardware-tailored noise profiling module using continuous weak measurement and machine learning-based noise classification to detect noise shifts within microseconds, (2) an adaptive QEC code selector that autonomously switches between lightweight QEC codes (e.g., tailored variants of the Bacon-Shor code and repetition codes) based on current noise characteristics, and (3) a quantum control feedback loop implemented on FPGA-based classical processors co-located with quantum hardware to minimize latency. This system dynamically balances error correction strength against qubit and time overhead, optimizing sensor accuracy under fluctuating noise.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on IBM Quantum Falcon and Rigetti Aspen-11 NISQ devices using the QVEC (Quantum Volume Error Correction) benchmark suite, the Quantum Hardware Error Dataset (QHED v2.0), and the NIST Quantum Sensor Noise Dataset. Metrics include (a) error correction latency (target <1 µs), (b) logical error rate reduction (>50% vs baseline QEC codes like surface code and repetition code), and (c) qubit overhead (target <40% increase over unencoded sensors). Perform ablation studies on noise profiling fidelity and adaptive code switching thresholds over 30 independent runs.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior low-overhead QEC codes or compiler optimizations that optimize for static noise or gate efficiency, this method uniquely integrates ultra-fast noise profiling with adaptive QEC code switching tailored to quantum sensors’ dynamic environments, achieving latency and overhead performance unattainable by existing frameworks focused on general NISQ devices or static noise models.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical multi-timescale adaptive quantum error correction (HTA-QEC) framework that integrates ultra-low-latency online noise profiling with predictive noise modeling to dynamically optimize QEC code selection and parameters across fast (sub-millisecond) and slow (second-to-minute) noise fluctuation regimes in NISQ edge devices.

BACKGROUND:  
The open problem addressed is that current adaptive quantum error correction methods, including the winning approach “Low-Latency Adaptive Quantum Error Correction via Online Noise Profiling,” only achieve rapid code adaptation at sub-millisecond timescales but fail to incorporate slower, more gradual noise drifts that degrade performance over longer durations. Existing static or single-timescale adaptive QEC schemes cannot optimally balance overhead and logical error suppression across multi-timescale noise dynamics inherent in NISQ devices, particularly in resource-constrained edge settings.

APPROACH:  
HTA-QEC continuously performs ultra-fast (sub-ms) online noise profiling combined with a lightweight predictive noise model trained on historical noise data, enabling hierarchical decision-making to select from a precompiled library of QEC codes and parameters optimized for different noise regimes and hardware constraints. The system incorporates a two-tier controller: a fast control loop that performs rapid code switching to handle transient noise spikes, and a slower supervisory loop that adjusts noise model parameters and code families based on longer-term trends. This method leverages Bayesian updating and reinforcement learning to refine noise predictions and code selection policies with minimal classical overhead, enabling robust logical error suppression across dynamically evolving noise environments.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HTA-QEC using the IBM Quantum Experience “ibmq_jakarta” and Rigetti Aspen-11 NISQ edge device noise profiles datasets, along with the Qiskit Aer noise model benchmark datasets. Baseline comparisons will be made against “Low-Latency Adaptive Quantum Error Correction via Online Noise Profiling,” the “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), and the “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026) frameworks. Metrics include average logical error rate reduction (>15% improvement over the best baseline), latency of QEC adaptation (<1 ms for fast loop), and classical computational overhead (under 5% CPU utilization on embedded classical processors). Experiments will run on quantum simulators with noise injection and on real hardware over 50 randomly seeded noise fluctuation sequences, with ablations isolating the impact of the predictive noise modeling and hierarchical adaptation mechanisms.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that achieves adaptation only on a single timescale or relies on static or slow-update schemes, HTA-QEC meaningfully surpasses them by integrating multi-timescale noise profiling and predictive modeling to enable continuous, hierarchical QEC optimization. This enables robust error correction that dynamically adapts both to rapid transient noise and slower drift, a capability missing from existing adaptive QEC methods limited to either static or purely reactive control, thereby significantly improving logical qubit fidelity and resource efficiency in practical edge NISQ deployments.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop an integrated error-aware quantum compiler framework that jointly optimizes qubit mapping, gate scheduling, and dynamic error mitigation by leveraging multi-objective global optimization over real device noise and connectivity models to significantly reduce logical error rates and improve circuit fidelity on NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND: Current NISQ devices face severe fidelity bottlenecks due to noise, limited qubit connectivity, and gate errors, which traditional quantum error correction codes cannot efficiently correct without prohibitive qubit overhead. Existing quantum compilers either separately optimize qubit mapping or gate synthesis without jointly balancing connectivity constraints and gate fidelity, resulting in suboptimal error resilience and scalability in large quantum simulations.

APPROACH: We propose a novel compiler design that formulates qubit mapping, gate scheduling, and error mitigation as a unified multi-objective global optimization problem, explicitly balancing connectivity-induced SWAP overhead and gate error rates estimated from empirical noise models of target devices (e.g., IBM Falcon r10 and Google Sycamore). The framework integrates state-of-the-art noise-aware heuristics with a Pareto optimization approach to minimize error propagation while preserving hardware constraints. Additionally, the method dynamically inserts targeted error mitigation gates and pulse-level calibrations within the compiler pipeline to further suppress logical errors.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark quantum circuits from the IBM Qiskit Benchmark suite, Google Quantum AI Benchmarks, and Rigetti’s Aspen benchmark set, comparing against baseline compilers including IBM Qiskit transpiler (default and noise-adaptive modes), Google Cirq’s routing optimization, and the t|ket> compiler. Metrics include achieving >3% absolute improvement in average circuit fidelity, >25% reduction in logical error rate as measured by randomized benchmarking, and 20% reduction in SWAP gate insertion overhead. Experiments will run on simulators with calibrated noise models and real quantum hardware where available, using 5 random seeds per benchmark and ablation studies isolating mapping, scheduling, and mitigation contributions.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work such as isolated error-resilient compiler designs or low-overhead QEC codes, our approach uniquely integrates global multi-objective optimization of qubit connectivity and gate fidelity with in-pipeline dynamic error mitigation, providing a holistic solution that meaningfully reduces error rates without the qubit overhead of traditional QEC or the fragmented optimization of existing compilers.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
A modular quantum error correction architecture leveraging hierarchical, locality-aware parallel syndrome extraction combined with adaptive, real-time classical feedback scheduling to reduce latency and qubit overhead in NISQ devices with 100–1000 qubits.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) approaches for large qubit arrays, including the winning scalable parallel syndrome extraction architecture, achieve latency reduction by exploiting locality and parallelism but do not fully address the adaptive management of classical processing bottlenecks and dynamic noise variability in NISQ devices. Existing QEC codes impose significant qubit overhead and static syndrome extraction schedules that limit responsiveness to fluctuating noise, impeding effective error suppression and scalability on near-term devices with constrained coherence times and gate fidelities.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical syndrome extraction framework that partitions large qubit arrays into modular clusters with local syndrome processors operating in parallel, while a higher-level adaptive classical scheduler dynamically prioritizes syndrome extraction and error correction cycles based on real-time noise and error rate feedback. This scheduler uses lightweight machine learning models embedded in the classical controller to predict error hotspots and reallocate processing resources, thereby minimizing overall latency and reducing qubit overhead by avoiding unnecessary syndrome extraction. The architecture supports hardware-efficient classical-quantum communication protocols and is designed for NISQ-era devices with 100–1000 qubits, ensuring gate fidelity remains above 99.9%.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted via high-fidelity quantum device simulators benchmarked on the Google Quantum AI Sycamore dataset and IBM Quantum Experience calibration data, alongside the Quantum Error Correction Benchmark Suite (QECBench). Metrics include syndrome extraction latency (target ≥40% reduction vs baseline), logical error rate (target ≤0.005 in surface code logical qubits), and qubit overhead (target ≤30% reduction compared to standard surface code implementations). Experiments will run with 5 independent random seeds per configuration on simulated arrays of 100, 500, and 1000 qubits under realistic noise models. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of hierarchical clustering and adaptive scheduling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike previous architectures that statically parallelize syndrome extraction and treat classical processing as a fixed bottleneck, this method jointly optimizes quantum and classical resources via an adaptive feedback loop, enabling dynamic noise-aware scheduling and qubit overhead reduction. This innovation goes beyond the winning architecture’s static modular design by integrating predictive classical control to further reduce latency and improve error suppression tailored to NISQ device noise dynamics.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive sparse coding framework optimized for neuromorphic hardware that dynamically tunes sparsity and memory footprint under intermittent connectivity and streaming data constraints to achieve ultra-low power edge IoT inference and continual learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Edge IoT devices face stringent power, memory, and latency constraints while processing continuous streaming data in real time, often with intermittent network connectivity. Existing sparse coding algorithms do not simultaneously optimize for adaptive sparsity control, low memory usage, and resilience to connectivity disruptions, resulting in excessive energy consumption and degraded learning performance on neuromorphic platforms. This bottleneck limits scalable, autonomous intelligence at the edge.

APPROACH:  
We propose a neuromorphic-compatible adaptive sparse coding algorithm that integrates: (1) a dynamic sparsity controller using reinforcement learning to minimize active neuron usage per input; (2) a memory-efficient incremental update rule tailored for streaming data with intermittent connectivity, leveraging local synaptic plasticity rules and compressed representation; and (3) hardware-aware approximation techniques exploiting event-driven, spike-based neuromorphic processors to reduce compute overhead. The method includes co-design of algorithm and hardware mapping to optimize energy-delay product under real-world edge constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the N-MNIST and DVS Gesture datasets for event-based vision processing, and the TIDIGITS spoken digit dataset for streaming audio. Baselines will include conventional sparse coding (Olshausen & Field 1997), Adaptive Sparse Coding (ASC-Edge 2023), and spiking neural network implementations such as Spiking ResNet and TrueNorth-compatible sparse coding. Metrics reported will include classification accuracy (>1.5% absolute improvement), average active neuron ratio reduction (>25% vs ASC-Edge), memory footprint reduction (>30%), and end-to-end energy consumption measured on Loihi 2 and Intel’s Dynap-SE2 neuromorphic chips (targeting >35% power reduction). Experiments will be run over 5 seeds, with ablation studies to isolate effects of adaptive sparsity control and incremental learning under connectivity disruptions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior neuromorphic sparse coding methods that assume stable connectivity and static sparsity levels, this work explicitly optimizes adaptive sparsity and memory efficiency for real-time streaming data on neuromorphic hardware under intermittent connectivity. It is the first to leverage reinforcement learning for dynamic sparsity control in neuromorphic sparse coding and co-design hardware approximations for ultra-low power edge inference and continual learning, closing the gap between algorithm adaptability and hardware constraints.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a neuromorphic algorithm-hardware co-design framework integrating ultra-low-power vertical dual-gate neurotransistors with adaptive spiking neural network (SNN) architectures optimized for real-time, always-on wearable health monitoring under strict energy, latency, and thermal constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing neuromorphic solutions for wearable health monitoring devices struggle to simultaneously meet the stringent power budgets, compact form factors, and real-time responsiveness required for continuous health data acquisition and inference. Current hardware synaptic devices (e.g., 2D materials-based or neurotransistor designs) and algorithms often fail to co-optimize across energy efficiency, latency, and thermal dissipation, limiting practical deployment in wearables with tight energy and size constraints. The open problem is achieving end-to-end system-level co-optimization that delivers real-time inference while sustaining ultra-low power consumption (<10 µW) and minimal thermal footprint (<1°C rise) in continuous wearable scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a co-designed system combining (1) novel vertical dual-gate electrolyte-gated neurotransistors with short-term plasticity and record-low read power (~3 fW) as hardware synaptic elements, and (2) an adaptive event-driven spiking neural network architecture that dynamically adjusts inference complexity based on sensed physiological signal variability. The hardware supports in-situ learning with hardware-implemented stochastic DropConnect to regularize and compress the network, reducing inference energy and latency. Algorithmically, the SNN layer-wise spiking thresholds and synaptic weight updates are co-optimized with device-level parameters to minimize power while maintaining classification accuracy and responsiveness. This synergy enables continuous, always-on operation with minimal energy and thermal cost on a compact wearable platform.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark physiological signal datasets: PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, WESAD (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection), and PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring. Baselines include Spiking ResNet-18, hardware-implemented DropConnect neuromorphic networks, and vertical dual-gate neurotransistor-based SNNs from prior work. Metrics include >2% absolute F1-score improvement for arrhythmia and activity recognition, latency reduction by ≥30% versus baseline neuromorphic inference, and system-level power consumption below 10 µW with thermal rise <1°C in a simulated wearable environment. Experimental setup includes training on GPUs with fixed compute budgets (~1 million training samples), hardware emulation on FPGA/neurotransistor arrays, and 5-fold cross-validation with ablation studies on hardware-software co-optimization components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that separately optimizes hardware synaptic devices or neuromorphic algorithms, this research achieves true algorithm-hardware co-design targeting ultra-low-power, real-time wearable health monitoring. It is the first to integrate vertical dual-gate neurotransistors’ short-term plasticity with adaptive SNN architectures leveraging hardware DropConnect for continuous, always-on inference under strict thermal and latency constraints—capabilities absent from existing 2D-material synapse or DropConnect implementations that do not co-optimize system-level wearable constraints.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an ultra-low-energy event-driven spiking neuromorphic architecture with integrated on-chip power interruption detection and adaptive state retention, optimized for battery-powered edge devices operating under intermittent power and stringent hardware constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neuromorphic architectures for battery-powered edge devices struggle to simultaneously achieve ultra-low energy consumption per event and robust operation under intermittent power conditions, leading to frequent data loss and system downtime. Existing optoelectronic synapses or ferroelectric devices optimize synaptic efficiency but lack mechanisms to maintain computational state during power interruptions, which critically limits autonomous, continuous edge intelligence in resource-constrained IoT and mobile platforms.

APPROACH:  
We propose a spiking neuromorphic system combining event-driven asynchronous neuron circuits with a novel low-overhead power interruption detection module that triggers on-chip state retention using non-volatile memristive crossbar arrays. The architecture employs adaptive event sparsity control to dynamically minimize energy-per-spike based on instantaneous battery status and workload demands. Integrated power-aware scheduling further ensures real-time responsiveness despite intermittent power, while the memristive state retention enables seamless recovery without retraining or reboot overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic visual and auditory benchmarks: N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and TIDIGITS datasets using spike-based encoding. Compare against IBM TrueNorth, Intel Loihi, and the Hardware-Implemented DropConnect ferroelectric synapse system. Metrics include average energy-per-event (target <10 pJ/event), power outage recovery time (<1 ms), and classification accuracy (>92% on N-MNIST). Perform 5 independent runs with ablations isolating state retention, event sparsity control, and power-aware scheduling modules. Training compute limited to 1000 GPU hours on NVIDIA RTX A6000.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works focusing solely on synaptic device efficiency or energy per spike, this research is the first to integrate hardware-aware power interruption detection with adaptive event sparsity and non-volatile state retention in a full spiking neuromorphic system tailored for battery-powered edge devices, enabling continuous, real-time intelligent processing under intermittent power—an open problem unaddressed by existing neuromorphic designs.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Design and demonstrate a latency-tail guaranteed analog in-memory compute architecture leveraging adaptive device-level variability compensation and hierarchical circuit timing control to enable real-time DNN inference under 1 ms latency constraints with >35% energy savings and minimal accuracy degradation on large-scale vision benchmarks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current analog in-memory computing (AIMC) accelerators for DNN inference often suffer from severe device non-idealities that induce unpredictable latency spikes, rendering them unreliable for latency-sensitive applications like autonomous driving. Existing AIMC methods focus mainly on average-case energy and accuracy trade-offs but lack mechanisms to explicitly bound worst-case tail latency, limiting their practical deployment in real-time systems requiring strict latency guarantees below 1 ms. This open problem necessitates a comprehensive cross-layer approach to tame latency tail distributions while maintaining high energy efficiency and accuracy on complex DNNs.

APPROACH:  
We propose an integrated solution combining (1) adaptive device-level variability compensation using in-situ calibration and real-time tuning of conductance states in AIMC arrays to stabilize operation speed, and (2) hierarchical circuit-level timing control that dynamically gates and pipelines sub-array operations to avoid tail latency spikes. The design employs a closed-loop feedback system that monitors latency tail percentiles and adjusts device biasing and timing margins to enforce worst-case latency constraints. This approach is validated on analog RRAM and PCM-based compute-in-memory arrays optimized for convolutional and transformer layers, supporting seamless integration with state-of-the-art DNN topologies.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on ImageNet-1K, CIFAR-100, and COCO object detection datasets using ResNet-50, MobileNetV3, and DETR networks as baselines. Quantitative metrics include maximum 99.9th percentile latency under 1 ms, energy per inference with a target >35% reduction versus digital baselines (NVIDIA A100 GPU, Google TPUv4), and top-1 accuracy degradation below 1.5% absolute. Experiments are conducted on custom AIMC test chips under varying temperature and voltage conditions, with ablations on compensation granularity and timing control policies, and repeated with 5 random seeds for statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior analog in-memory compute designs which lack explicit latency tail control and rely mostly on average energy-accuracy trade-offs, our approach provides the first demonstrated device-to-circuit closed-loop system for worst-case latency-bound enforcement in AIMC. This ensures deterministic real-time inference with guaranteed sub-ms latency, a capability absent in state-of-the-art neuromorphic and AIMC accelerators that has so far prevented their deployment in critical low-latency settings.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) learning algorithm with dynamic power-memory tradeoff control for real-time, energy-efficient neuromorphic learning on resource-constrained edge IoT devices.

BACKGROUND: Edge IoT devices require continuous on-device learning from streaming sensor data under stringent power and memory constraints. Existing neuromorphic learning algorithms employing fixed STDP rules either consume excessive energy or adapt too slowly to environmental changes, limiting their utility in always-on, autonomous edge scenarios. The key open problem is to enable fast, efficient adaptation without exceeding tight energy budgets or memory limits, which current fixed or hardware-intensive plasticity mechanisms cannot achieve.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive STDP framework that dynamically modulates synaptic update frequency and precision based on real-time energy availability and memory usage, using lightweight on-chip controllers integrated with neuromorphic hardware. The method leverages mixed-precision synaptic weight representations and selective synapse activation schedules driven by stochastic event-triggered updates to minimize redundant computations. Additionally, a feedback loop adjusts learning parameters in response to environmental volatility detected via sensor data statistics, optimizing plasticity responsiveness within power-memory budgets.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on neuromorphic real-time temporal pattern recognition tasks using the N-MNIST spiking dataset, the DVS Gesture dataset, and the Intel Neuromorphic Sensor dataset. Baselines include fixed-parameter STDP implementations, the Adaptive Synaptic Plasticity (ASP) model, and standard SpikeProp learning. Metrics target >15% reduction in energy consumption (measured via Joules per inference), >25% reduction in on-chip memory footprint, and latency improvements of at least 20% in adaptation time while maintaining classification accuracy within 2% of baselines. Experiments run on Intel Loihi 2 and TrueNorth neuromorphic chips, with 5 random initialization seeds and ablations on adaptive parameters.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior neuromorphic STDP algorithms that use static plasticity rules or hardware-fixed synaptic precision, this approach introduces a real-time adaptive mechanism explicitly balancing energy and memory constraints with learning speed, enabling efficient continuous learning on severely resource-limited IoT edge devices—a capability not addressed by existing neuromorphic or optoelectronic synaptic devices.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable, adaptive generative causal model architecture tailored for continuous, high-velocity multivariate sensor streams in Industrial IoT, that explicitly models nonstationarity, feedback loops, and sensor noise to enable real-time, robust counterfactual inference and diagnostics.

BACKGROUND: Industrial IoT systems generate complex continuous sensor data exhibiting nonstationarity, nonlinear causal feedback, and significant noise, challenging existing causal discovery and generative causal models, which lack scalability and robustness for real-time counterfactual inference. Current state-of-the-art methods such as KiNCD and S-FCI handle nonlinearities and multi-environment data but do not address the combined challenges of high-frequency streaming data, nonstationarity adaptation, and real-time counterfactual simulation fidelity necessary for industrial diagnostics and predictive maintenance.

APPROACH: We propose a novel hybrid architecture combining a streaming-adapted Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) based nonlinear causal discovery module with a continuous-time generative causal model that incorporates dynamic causal graph updates via online structure learning and robust noise modeling using state-space sensor noise filtering. The model will incorporate modular feedback loop detection and integrate domain-informed inductive biases for nonstationarity adaptation through temporal attention mechanisms. Real-time counterfactual inference is enabled by amortized inference networks optimized for streaming updates, maintaining scalability to high-dimensional sensor arrays and continuous data flows.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the NASA Prognostics Center Turbofan Engine Degradation Simulation Dataset, the Intel Lab Data (sensor streams from environmental monitoring), and the BEAR dataset (bearing fault sensor data). Baselines will include KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search (OSS). Metrics include >3% absolute improvement in Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for causal graph accuracy, a reduction of at least 25% in counterfactual inference latency, and >5% improvement in fault diagnosis F1 score. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with training budgets of up to 48 hours per dataset, averaged over 5 random seeds, including ablation studies on noise filtering and feedback detection components.

NOVELTY: Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, our method explicitly addresses continuous streaming data with dynamic, adaptive causal structure learning under nonstationarity and noisy sensor conditions, enabling real-time counterfactual simulations that prior models cannot scale to or robustly maintain. This integrated approach to feedback loop modeling and noise filtering in an industrial IoT generative causal framework is novel and critical for operational diagnostics.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, incremental latent causal discovery framework optimized for edge healthcare devices that efficiently uncovers nonlinear causal structures from streaming, noisy patient data with constrained memory and compute, enabling real-time on-device diagnostics.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing causal discovery methods either demand extensive computation and memory, making them infeasible on edge healthcare devices, or rely on batch processing unsuitable for continuous, heterogeneous patient data streams. The open problem is to create a causal discovery approach that performs robust, interpretable nonlinear latent causal inference incrementally under strict resource constraints, enabling timely personalized diagnostics directly on edge devices without cloud reliance.

APPROACH:  
We propose EdgeLatentCausal, a hybrid model combining a compact latent variable causal graph representation with a lightweight nonlinear function approximator based on sparse KAN (Kolmogorov-Arnold Network) modules, enabling efficient modeling of complex dependencies. An incremental update mechanism integrates incoming patient data streams, updating causal graphs and latent variables on-device in real-time with bounded memory usage. The method incorporates noise-robust regularization and continual pruning strategies to maintain interpretability and robustness under heterogeneous conditions while adhering to edge device constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate EdgeLatentCausal on real-world healthcare time-series datasets including MIMIC-III, PhysioNet Challenge 2012, and the eICU Collaborative Research Database. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and amortized causal discovery methods. Metrics will measure causal graph structural accuracy (Structural Hamming Distance, >10% improvement), inference robustness under noisy inputs (AUROC on downstream diagnostic tasks, >5% increase), and runtime latency/memory footprint (reduce latency by ≥30%, memory by ≥40% versus baselines) on representative edge compute hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano). Experiments will entail 5 seeds with ablations on update frequency and pruning thresholds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI that require batch processing and heavy compute or amortized models that lack incremental update mechanisms, EdgeLatentCausal uniquely enables continuous latent causal discovery and adaptation on resource-limited edge devices with provable robustness to noise and heterogeneity, supporting real-time personalized healthcare diagnostics without cloud dependency.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive causal discovery framework that leverages regime shift detection via change-point analysis combined with continual learning of nonlinear causal graphs, enabling robust and timely updates of causal structure inference in financial markets under non-stationary economic regimes.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods in financial domains assume stationarity or rely on static multi-environment data, failing to promptly detect and adapt to abrupt regime shifts such as policy changes or crises that invalidate prior causal models. This leads to outdated or incorrect causal inferences, severely impacting downstream tasks like risk management and policy evaluation. The open problem is designing a method that dynamically detects regime changes and continuously updates causal structures in a computationally efficient manner for real-world financial time series.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-stage framework: (1) Incorporate advanced online change-point detection algorithms (e.g., kernel-based or Bayesian online changepoint detection) to identify regime shifts in multivariate financial time series data, and (2) apply a continual nonlinear causal discovery algorithm inspired by KiNCD but extended with adaptive weighting and memory mechanisms to re-learn causal graphs incrementally and robustly after detected shifts. This includes enforcing invariant conditional distributions over stable regimes and adjusting model complexity dynamically in response to data non-stationarity. The method integrates domain-specific financial constraints (e.g., no-arbitrage, temporal ordering) to enhance causal validity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark financial causal discovery datasets including the FRED-MD macroeconomic dataset, the CRSP daily stock returns dataset, and the Global Financial Crisis dataset (capturing 2007-2009 regime changes). Baselines include KiNCD (2025), S-FCI (2023), and the Adaptive Causal Discovery method from the tournament winner paper. Metrics include Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) improvement by >15% post-regime shifts, F1-score increase in causal edge recovery by >10%, and regime detection delay reduced below 5 trading days. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablations on regime detection accuracy and continual update frequency.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI which assume static or multiple fixed environments, our method explicitly couples real-time regime shift detection with continual causal graph adaptation, enabling up-to-date and valid causal inference under evolving financial conditions. Prior adaptive methods lack robust online change-point integration and nonlinear continual learning mechanisms tailored to financial constraints, which this work addresses concretely and with falsifiable performance gains.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a robust causal discovery framework that jointly models adversarial perturbations and detects regime shifts in high-dimensional financial time series using a novel regime-aware adversarial training mechanism integrated with nonlinear causal structure learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing causal discovery methods applied to financial markets often fail under volatile conditions due to adversarial noise and sudden regime changes, causing false causal edges or missed dependencies that impair decision-making. Current approaches either address adversarial robustness or regime change detection separately but do not integrate these challenges jointly in a scalable, nonlinear causal discovery framework. There is a critical need for a method that can simultaneously handle adversarial perturbations and regime segmentation to reliably infer causal relationships in nonstationary, noisy financial data.

APPROACH:  
We propose Regime-Adversarial Nonlinear Causal Discovery (RANCD), a framework that combines (1) a nonlinear causal structure learner based on a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) backbone for expressive modeling of complex dependencies, (2) an adversarial noise generation module trained jointly via a minimax objective to simulate worst-case perturbations typical in financial crises, and (3) a regime segmentation network using a hierarchical hidden Markov model to detect and incorporate regime changes explicitly during causal edge estimation. The joint training optimizes causal graph structure, adversarial robustness, and regime segmentation end-to-end, ensuring stable edge identification and predictive accuracy across market regimes and perturbations.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark financial datasets with annotated regime changes: (1) FRED-MD macroeconomic dataset, (2) S&P 500 daily returns with regime labels from the Economic Policy Uncertainty Index, and (3) FX market data with volatility regime annotations. Baselines include KiNCD (2025), S-FCI (2023), and Adversarial Causal Discovery (ACD) methods from the tournament winner’s original paper. Metrics to measure: (a) Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) with expected >10% absolute improvement over baselines in stable edge recovery during volatile regimes, (b) average predictive log-likelihood on held-out data with >5% improvement, and (c) regime detection F1-score above 0.8. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with at least 5 random seeds, including ablations removing adversarial or regime components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI which do not consider adversarial noise or regime dynamics explicitly, and prior adversarial causal discovery methods that lack nonlinear modeling or regime segmentation, RANCD uniquely integrates adversarial training with nonlinear causal learning and explicit regime segmentation in a unified optimization framework. This enables robust causal inference validated across diverse financial regimes and adversarial perturbations, a capability absent in existing state-of-the-art methods.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a memory- and compute-efficient continual causal discovery framework that leverages adaptive nonlinear latent factor pruning combined with multi-environment invariant causal representation learning from purely observational streaming data on edge devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods either require large memory buffers and offline training (e.g., deep latent causal disentanglement models) or rely on interventional/multi-environment data, which are unavailable in many real-world streaming scenarios such as continuous healthcare monitoring on edge devices. Existing streaming causal factor discovery approaches like the winning method only apply linear or shallow representations, lack robustness to nonlinear relationships, and do not leverage multi-environment invariance to improve causal identifiability under strict memory constraints (<100MB). Thus, a method is needed that can do online nonlinear causal discovery from streaming purely observational data with limited memory on embedded hardware while maintaining robustness to distributional shifts.

APPROACH:  
We propose a continual learning causal discovery framework that integrates: (1) a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-based nonlinear latent causal factor model for expressive nonlinear representation learning; (2) an adaptive latent factor pruning mechanism inspired by the Memory-Efficient Streaming Causal Factor Discovery approach to compress causal factors dynamically under strict memory bounds; (3) a multi-environment invariant causal regularizer that enforces consistent causal structure discovery across changing, unlabeled streaming data distributions to enhance identifiability; (4) an online, memory-efficient optimization protocol tailored for edge hardware with <100MB RAM and low compute footprint, incorporating continual learning strategies to prevent catastrophic forgetting. This approach operates solely on observational streaming data without requiring interventions or offline retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the PhysioNet Sepsis Challenge dataset for healthcare time-series streaming data, the DREAM3 In Silico Gene Network dataset for nonlinear causal gene regulatory networks, and the CauseMe benchmark for synthetic nonlinear causal graphs with distribution shifts. Metrics include causal factor reconstruction error (target <10% improvement vs baselines), Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) of discovered causal graphs (target >15% reduction), and memory footprint capped below 100MB. Baselines: Memory-Efficient Streaming Causal Factor Discovery, KiNCD, and S-FCI. Experiments run on embedded ARM hardware simulators with 5 random seeds, including ablations on pruning strategy and invariant regularization.

NOVELTY:  
This method is the first to combine nonlinear KAN-based causal factorization with adaptive latent pruning and multi-environment invariant causal regularization in a streaming, memory-limited continual learning setting on edge hardware. Prior work either lacks nonlinear expressivity, multi-environment invariance, or memory efficiency for embedded deployment, making this approach uniquely positioned for robust, continuous causal discovery from purely observational, non-stationary streaming data under severe resource constraints.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-optimized, multi-objective graph generative model for on-device molecular design that jointly balances ultra-low latency (<30ms inference), high chemical validity (>95%), and target-specific bioactivity prediction by integrating lightweight multi-task learning and advanced model compression techniques.

BACKGROUND: Current state-of-the-art graph generative models for molecular design, such as the reported latency-optimized graph neural network, achieve sub-50ms inference latency and good chemical validity but lack integration of explicit bioactivity prediction or target-specific optimization within the ultra-low latency constraint required for real-time, point-of-care drug discovery devices. Existing approaches either prioritize molecular validity and generation speed or focus separately on drug-target affinity prediction, which hampers practical deployment for fast therapeutic candidate generation tailored to specific disease targets on edge hardware.

APPROACH: We propose a novel graph neural network architecture that unifies generative molecular graph construction with lightweight, on-device multi-task learning to concurrently generate valid molecules and predict their binding affinities to predefined biological targets. The model employs structured pruning, quantization-aware training, and knowledge distillation from larger multi-task networks to compress the architecture aggressively without sacrificing chemical validity or bioactivity prediction accuracy. Additionally, a novel latency-aware loss function ensures the model respects strict inference time budgets below 30ms on typical ARM-based edge devices. During training, we incorporate target-specific bioactivity data enabling goal-directed molecular design aligned with therapeutic needs.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be performed on three benchmark molecular datasets: QM9 (quantum chemical properties), MOSES (molecular generation quality), and ChEMBL (bioactivity profiles for drug-target interactions). Baselines include the Latency-Optimized Graph Generative Model (2025), GraphVAE, and JT-VAE. Metrics include inference latency (target <30ms on ARM Cortex-A72 CPU), chemical validity (>95%), uniqueness (>90%), Fréchet ChemNet Distance (FCD) improvement by ≥10%, and bioactivity prediction ROC-AUC >0.85 for target-specific tasks. Experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with quantization-aware fine-tuning, running 5 seeds for statistical significance, and ablations on pruning granularity and multi-task loss weighting.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that optimize only molecular validity and latency or focus on bioactivity prediction separately, this approach meaningfully integrates multi-task bioactivity prediction within a highly compressed, latency-constrained graph generative framework tailored for edge deployment. It enables real-time, target-guided molecular design on portable devices, a capability unmet by current models that either exceed latency requirements or lack target-specific optimization in ultra-low latency settings.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable physics-informed generative AI framework integrating differentiable molecular force fields with graph-based architectures to enable ultra-high-throughput virtual screening of billions of compounds with accuracy comparable to full molecular dynamics simulations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current virtual screening methods either rely on fast but physically approximate scoring functions that lack molecular interaction fidelity or on accurate physics-based simulations that are too computationally intensive for large-scale screening. This tradeoff creates a bottleneck limiting early-stage drug discovery throughput and accuracy, especially when screening ultra-large chemical libraries. The open problem is to design a generative model that balances physical accuracy and computational efficiency without sacrificing either, enabling rapid and reliable identification of promising drug candidates at unprecedented scale.

APPROACH:  
We propose a graph neural network (GNN) generative model conditioned on differentiable physics-informed energy potentials derived from coarse-grained molecular force fields, trained jointly with a surrogate model of quantum mechanical properties. The framework uses a multi-fidelity loss combining physics-based energy predictions and ligand-target binding affinity surrogates, enabling rapid molecule generation and scoring without explicit MD simulations. We optimize the architecture for parallel inference on GPU clusters, incorporating a scalable approximate message passing scheme to maintain accuracy while screening billions of molecules. The method is benchmarked with iterative active learning cycles to refine physical potentials using a small set of high-fidelity molecular dynamics simulations.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on the ZINC15, ChEMBL v29, and PubChem BioAssay datasets, comparing against baselines including GNINA docking, PhysNet, and MolDQN. Metrics include docking score correlation (>0.85 Pearson with gold-standard MD), throughput (>10^9 molecules/day on 100 GPUs), and hit enrichment at top-1% (>15% improvement over GNINA).
