Experimental protocols include training on 500 million molecules with 3 random seeds, ablations on physics loss components, and active learning impact assessment.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work such as “High-Throughput Virtual Screening with Physics-Informed Generative Models,” our method explicitly integrates differentiable molecular force fields with graph-based generative models and quantum property surrogates, enabling orders-of-magnitude improvement in throughput without sacrificing molecular interaction accuracy. This approach surpasses purely data-driven or purely physics-based methods by jointly optimizing molecular generation and physics-informed scoring in a scalable end-to-end framework.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, on-device hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that optimizes multi-objective molecular properties in real-time under strict resource constraints, enabling decentralized and privacy-preserving drug discovery workflows on edge devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current molecular optimization methods require substantial computational resources and are unsuitable for deployment on portable, low-power hardware, hindering real-time, decentralized drug design particularly in resource-limited settings. Existing on-device reinforcement learning approaches for molecular optimization focus primarily on single-objective optimization and lack mechanisms to efficiently handle complex multi-objective tasks while maintaining low memory and compute footprints.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) architecture combining a compressed latent molecular representation via a variational autoencoder (VAE) with a lightweight, modular policy network optimized for edge deployment. The framework will use multi-objective reward shaping to balance drug-likeness, synthesizability, and target-specific binding affinity. Efficient online learning algorithms with experience replay and dynamic compression will enable continual adaptation on-device without cloud dependency. The system will be implemented and benchmarked on ARM Cortex-M class microcontrollers and edge GPUs to ensure operation within strict memory (<256MB), compute (<5W), and latency (<100ms per molecule) budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on benchmark molecular datasets including ZINC250K, ChEMBL, and the MOSES dataset, optimizing for multiple drug discovery objectives. Baselines will include REINVENT, MolDQN, and the previously published On-Device Reinforcement Learning for Molecular Optimization framework. Metrics will include multi-objective optimization success rate (>10% improvement in Pareto front coverage), inference latency reduction (>30% vs On-Device RL baseline), and on-device memory footprint (<256MB). Experiments will run across 5 random seeds with ablations on hierarchical structure and compression strategies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that focuses on single-objective molecular optimization or requires cloud resources, this method uniquely integrates hierarchical RL and compressed latent representations for efficient multi-objective molecular design directly on resource-constrained devices, enabling real-time, decentralized, and privacy-preserving drug discovery workflows inaccessible to existing approaches.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Multi-Objective Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning framework that integrates domain-adaptive molecular representation learning with real-time multi-property feedback to achieve ultra-fast, simultaneous optimization of drug leads for efficacy, toxicity, and pharmacokinetics within industrial time constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current lead optimization methods either optimize drug properties sequentially or rely on single-objective reinforcement learning, which fails to meet the stringent time budgets and complex multi-property trade-offs required in industrial drug discovery pipelines. Existing approaches lack adaptive molecular representations that evolve alongside optimization and cannot efficiently handle high-dimensional, conflicting objectives in real-time, leading to delayed lead progression and suboptimal candidate selection.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework where a high-level agent adaptively selects optimization objectives and priorities based on real-time feedback, while low-level agents generate molecular modifications using a domain-adaptive graph neural network (GNN) encoder-decoder trained on both global datasets and local domain-specific data. The system concurrently optimizes for efficacy, toxicity, and ADMET properties by leveraging efficient Pareto-front approximation with multi-armed bandit strategies to dynamically balance exploration-exploitation. Molecular representations are continuously fine-tuned during optimization to capture evolving chemical space characteristics, enabling faster convergence and improved property trade-offs under fixed computational budgets typical of industrial workflows.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets Tox21, ChEMBL, and ADMET Benchmark Suite to measure Pareto front quality (hypervolume indicator), runtime efficiency (average optimization time per lead ≤ 2 hours), and multi-objective score improvements (≥ 10% gain in combined efficacy-toxicity metrics) compared to baselines. Baselines include the original Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning method from the tournament winner, GraphAF, and MolDQN. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per setup, including ablation studies on adaptive representation learning and hierarchical policy components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach uniquely integrates hierarchical reinforcement learning with adaptive molecular representation fine-tuning and dynamic objective prioritization, enabling rapid, simultaneous multi-property optimization that strictly adheres to industrial runtime constraints. Previous methods lack this combination of real-time molecular embedding adaptation and hierarchical decision-making for multi-objective trade-offs, making this method both faster and more effective in navigating high-dimensional chemical spaces with conflicting objectives.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-informed graph generative model that simultaneously optimizes molecular structures for oral bioavailability by integrating differentiable permeability constraints with multi-objective molecular property predictors, enabling end-to-end generation of drug candidates that satisfy permeability, metabolic stability, and binding affinity criteria within the generation loop.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI models for drug discovery inadequately incorporate membrane permeability constraints during molecule generation, often resulting in candidates with poor oral bioavailability that fail downstream ADME (absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion) profiling. Existing approaches typically apply permeability and ADME filters post hoc, causing inefficiencies and high attrition rates in virtual screening. The open problem is how to embed reliable, physics-informed permeability constraints directly into the generative process to produce orally deliverable molecules from the outset.

APPROACH:  
We propose a graph neural network-based generative model that integrates differentiable, physics-informed constraints derived from membrane permeability simulations (e.g., molecular dynamics or implicit membrane models) within the generation loop. The model will incorporate multi-task predictors trained on permeability (e.g., PAMPA), metabolic stability, and docking affinity scores, using a multi-objective loss function to guide molecule generation toward simultaneously optimizing these properties. The permeability constraint will be formulated as a differentiable penalty informed by predictive surrogate models calibrated on high-quality permeability simulation data, enabling backpropagation through the generation steps. Reinforcement learning fine-tuning will refine candidates to balance oral bioavailability with target binding efficacy.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train and evaluate the model on the ADMET benchmark dataset (which includes measured permeability and metabolic stability data), the ChEMBL dataset for bioactivity labels, and the PDBbind dataset for docking affinity validation. Baselines will include the physics-informed graph generative model from the tournament winner, GraphVAE, and JT-VAE. Metrics to assess improvements will be: (1) >5% absolute increase in predicted oral bioavailability scores, (2) >10% rise in docking success rates (binding affinity thresholds), and (3) improved metabolic stability classification accuracy by at least 4%. Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~10,000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds and ablation studies on the permeability constraint and multi-objective loss components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior models that apply ADME and permeability constraints as post-hoc filters or rely on implicit, single-objective optimization, our approach embeds differentiable, physics-informed permeability constraints directly into an end-to-end graph generative model while simultaneously optimizing multiple pharmacokinetic and binding properties. This enables the generation of oral drug candidates with balanced ADME and efficacy profiles, a capability absent in existing state-of-the-art frameworks that treat permeability superficially or separately from binding affinity.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical lightweight meta-learning framework that dynamically adapts federated models on resource-constrained IoT devices by jointly optimizing robust meta-initializations and communication-efficient split model architectures tailored for heterogeneous, non-IID sensor data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning approaches struggle with heterogeneous IoT clients due to device resource constraints and highly non-IID, sparse, noisy sensor data, leading to slow adaptation and degraded model performance. Existing meta-learning methods often involve complex local fine-tuning, unsuitable for ultra-low-power devices, while Split Federated Learning reduces computation but suffers from high communication costs and cannot adequately address statistical heterogeneity across clients. The open problem is how to enable fast, reliable, and communication-efficient federated adaptation on heterogeneous, resource-limited IoT sensors with non-IID data.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Lightweight Meta-Split Federated Learning (HLMSFL), combining (1) a novel meta-learning algorithm that learns robust meta-initializations optimized for sparse, noisy sensor data and minimal local updates, and (2) a split model architecture dynamically partitioned between client and server to minimize local computation and communication overhead. HLMSFL incorporates a client heterogeneity-aware scheduler that adapts both meta-initializations and split points per device capability and data distribution, enabling fast adaptation with fewer local tuning steps under intermittent connectivity. Additionally, we integrate a noise-robust loss function and adaptive communication compression techniques to further reduce bandwidth usage without accuracy loss.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HLMSFL on three real-world federated IoT datasets with heterogeneous and non-IID characteristics: Intel Lab Data (indoor sensor network), PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, and the WISDM Smartphone Activity Recognition dataset. Baselines for comparison include FedGKD (2021), HeteroSFL (2025), and the Lightweight Meta-Learning method from the tournament winner. Metrics include test accuracy/F1-score (target >3% absolute improvement over baselines), adaptation latency (reduce by ≥40%), and communication cost (reduce transmitted data volume by ≥35%). Experiments will be conducted on simulated IoT edge clusters with limited CPU/memory profiles over 5 seeds, including ablations on meta-initialization robustness and split architecture configurations.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods, HLMSFL explicitly integrates meta-learning with dynamic split federated architectures optimized jointly for heterogeneous IoT constraints and non-IID data, enabling both statistical and system heterogeneity adaptation at once. Prior SFL approaches do not optimize meta-initializations for fast adaptation on noisy, sparse data, nor dynamically adjust model splits per device and data variance, which HLMSFL addresses to achieve significant improvements in both accuracy and efficiency.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
We propose a Hierarchical Federated Clustering framework with Dynamic Client Adaptation (HFC-DCA) that robustly maintains stable, latency-aware clusters in mobile edge networks with highly dynamic client availability and heterogeneous, non-IID data, enabling faster convergence and higher accuracy than existing federated clustering and scheduling methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning approaches in mobile edge networks suffer from instability in cluster memberships and delayed model aggregation due to device mobility and fluctuating network conditions. Existing hierarchical federated clustering methods assume static or slowly changing client sets, which leads to degraded convergence speed and accuracy in latency-sensitive applications with heterogeneous, non-IID data distributions. The open problem is to design a federated clustering system that adaptively and efficiently handles dynamic client availability and data heterogeneity without compromising model performance or communication overhead.

APPROACH:  
HFC-DCA constructs and continuously updates a multi-level federation hierarchy using real-time network state and client availability metrics. It integrates adaptive cluster re-assignment strategies based on client mobility prediction and bandwidth estimation, combined with a heterogeneity-aware clustering criterion that groups clients by data distribution similarity and computational capacity. We incorporate a latency-aware aggregation scheduler that prioritizes timely model updates from dynamic clusters to minimize staleness and optimize convergence. Additionally, a lightweight meta-learning module personalizes cluster reconfiguration policies to balance exploration of new clusters and exploitation of stable memberships.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate HFC-DCA on CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and Shakespeare datasets to represent image and natural language non-IID federated scenarios with heterogeneous client capabilities. Baselines include Hierarchical Federated Clustering (HFC) [2023], FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation) [2021], and SC (Joint Client Scheduling and Wireless Resource Allocation) [2024]. Metrics measured are top-1 accuracy improvement (>3% absolute), convergence rounds reduction (≥25%), and latency reduction in aggregation (≥30%) compared to baselines. Experiments will be conducted on a simulated mobile edge environment emulating client mobility and bandwidth fluctuation over 5 seeds, with ablations on cluster adaptation frequency and heterogeneity sensitivity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as HFC [2023], which assume static or slowly changing client sets and do not explicitly optimize for latency or data heterogeneity, HFC-DCA dynamically adapts cluster memberships in real-time using client mobility and network conditions while explicitly incorporating data and device heterogeneity into clustering decisions. This enables stable, low-latency aggregation and faster convergence in truly dynamic edge scenarios where prior methods fail to maintain cluster stability or timely model updates.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a fault-tolerant, decentralized asynchronous federated learning framework with adaptive client clustering and dynamic model aggregation tailored to heterogeneous devices and highly non-IID data distributions, incorporating failure-aware update routing and personalized model calibration to maintain convergence and accuracy despite unpredictable device failures.

BACKGROUND:  
Current decentralized federated learning methods assume static client availability and often treat device heterogeneity and non-IID data challenges separately from fault tolerance, leading to degraded model quality or instability when devices fail or drop out unpredictably. Moreover, existing approaches lack mechanisms to adaptively cluster heterogeneous clients and dynamically recalibrate model aggregation in the presence of failures, which is critical for industrial IoT and edge networks with diverse hardware and skewed data. This work addresses the open problem of maintaining efficient, accurate, and stable decentralized FL on heterogeneous, non-IID data in environments with frequent, unpredictable device failures.

APPROACH:  
We propose an adaptive fault-tolerant decentralized federated learning system that (1) integrates failure detection with dynamic client clustering based on device capability and data distribution similarity, (2) employs failure-aware asynchronous update routing that re-routes model updates away from failed or slow nodes, and (3) introduces a personalized model aggregation mechanism that dynamically calibrates global consensus weights within clusters to mitigate client drift from non-IID data. The framework also incorporates a lightweight recovery protocol that uses local-gradient caching and redundancy to quickly restore learning progress without full resynchronization. The method runs peer-to-peer without reliance on a central server, leveraging consensus algorithms robust to node churn.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three benchmark datasets: **FEMNIST** (for heterogeneous non-IID image classification), **CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet non-IID partitioning**, and **N-BaIoT** (a real industrial IoT dataset with device failure logs). Baselines include **Fault-Tolerant Decentralized FL (FT-DFL)** from the tournament winner paper, **HeteroSFL** (2025), and **FedGKD** (2021). Metrics will include global model accuracy (targeting >3% improvement vs FT-DFL on FEMNIST), system stability measured by recovery time after failure (reduce recovery latency by 40%), and communication overhead (keep within 10% of baseline decentralized FL). Experiments run on an emulated edge network with 50 heterogeneous clients, 5 random failure injection scenarios per run, repeated over 5 seeds, including ablation studies on clustering and recovery components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior fault-tolerant FL methods that assume static client sets or homogeneous data, this approach uniquely combines adaptive clustering based on data and device heterogeneity with failure-aware asynchronous routing and personalized aggregation, enabling robust learning under simultaneous non-IID data skew and unpredictable device failures—capabilities that HeteroSFL, FedGKD, and FT-DFL cannot jointly deliver.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a communication- and computation-efficient meta-learning framework for wearable health federated learning that enables rapid personalization on highly heterogeneous, noisy, and non-IID data with minimal communication rounds and lightweight local updates.

BACKGROUND:  
Wearable health devices generate highly personalized, noisy, and sparse data under strict privacy constraints, resulting in severe statistical heterogeneity and resource limitations on client devices. Existing federated meta-learning approaches fail to rapidly adapt to individual user data distributions without incurring high communication overhead or requiring substantial local computation, leading to slow convergence and poor personalization on resource-constrained devices. The open problem is how to design federated meta-learning methods that simultaneously minimize communication and computation costs while maintaining fast adaptation and robustness to noise and non-IID data.

APPROACH:  
We propose MetaWearFL, a federated meta-learning algorithm tailored for wearable health applications that integrates (1) a lightweight meta-optimizer based on first-order MAML variants combined with noise-robust gradient estimators, (2) a novel adaptive communication scheduler that selectively uploads compressed model updates only when client drift exceeds a learned threshold, and (3) personalized local fine-tuning using a small subset of highly informative data identified via uncertainty sampling. This synergy reduces communication rounds by >40% and accelerates personalization by enabling fast adaptation on-device with minimal computation and enhanced noise resilience.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate MetaWearFL on three benchmark health datasets reflecting heterogeneous, non-IID wearable data: PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, MIMIC-III vital signs dataset, and the WESAD stress detection dataset. Baseline comparisons will include Per-FedAvg, FedGKD, and HeteroSFL. Metrics include task accuracy (target >2-3% absolute improvement over baselines), personalization speed measured by adaptation time per client (<30% of baseline wall clock time), and communication cost (target >40% reduction in bytes transmitted). Experiments will run on simulated edge hardware with 5 seeds for statistical significance, including ablations on communication scheduling and noise-robust updates.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior federated meta-learning and heterogeneous FL methods that either incur high communication overhead (HeteroSFL) or slow adaptation on noisy, personalized data (Per-FedAvg, FedGKD), MetaWearFL uniquely combines noise-robust gradient meta-optimization with adaptive communication scheduling and uncertainty-driven local fine-tuning to enable fast, lightweight, and privacy-preserving personalization tailored specifically to resource-constrained wearable health devices.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Latency- and data-distribution-aware dynamic graph aggregation for federated learning that jointly optimizes client similarity, latency constraints, and data heterogeneity to accelerate convergence without accuracy loss in highly heterogeneous and non-IID environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning on heterogeneous clients with non-IID data suffers from slow convergence and stale model updates due to network latency variability and statistical heterogeneity. Existing graph-based aggregation methods, including the winning Latency-Constrained Graph Aggregation, optimize for latency alone but ignore the impact of client data distribution heterogeneity on aggregation quality, leading to suboptimal model accuracy or slower convergence. Moreover, prior work lacks dynamic adaptation of aggregation neighborhoods that jointly consider latency budgets and statistical similarity, leaving the open problem of balancing these conflicting constraints in real-world heterogeneous FL scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel Latency- and Data-Aware Graph Aggregation (LDGA) algorithm that dynamically constructs client similarity graphs weighted by a combined metric incorporating both client-to-client latency and statistical heterogeneity measures (e.g., Wasserstein distance between local data distributions). LDGA adapts neighborhood sizes per client under explicit latency budgets to minimize aggregation latency while maximizing similarity in data distribution, thus improving gradient alignment during aggregation. To further reduce staleness from slow clients, LDGA employs asynchronous partial aggregation rounds prioritized by latency and distribution similarity scores. The approach integrates with existing FL pipelines by augmenting the aggregation step with the dual-constraint graph optimization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate LDGA on CIFAR-100, Google Speech Commands, and FEMNIST datasets representing vision, speech, and handwriting domains with realistic non-IID partitions. Baselines include Latency-Constrained Graph Aggregation (LCGA), FedGKD, HeteroSFL, and SC-WA (joint client scheduling and resource allocation). Metrics measured are top-1 accuracy (target >2% absolute improvement over LCGA), average aggregation latency reduction (>30% vs LCGA), and convergence speed (number of rounds to reach 90% peak accuracy, target 20% faster convergence). Experiments run on a simulated heterogeneous network cluster with 50 clients, repeated over 5 seeds, with ablation studies on latency vs distribution weighting and synchronous vs asynchronous aggregation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that optimize either latency or data heterogeneity alone, LDGA uniquely fuses latency and statistical distribution similarity into a unified graph aggregation framework, enabling principled dual-constraint neighborhood adaptation and asynchronous aggregation to handle real-world client heterogeneity. This joint optimization of communication latency and data skew is absent from existing SOTA approaches, yielding faster convergence and higher accuracy without sacrificing latency.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a Multi-Scale Graph Transformer Neural Operator (MSGT-NO) that jointly learns physics-preserving neural operators and dynamic grid topology embeddings to enable robust climate model emulation across heterogeneous, irregular, and dynamically refined variable-resolution meshes.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators largely fail to generalize across irregular and variable-resolution grids with dynamic mesh refinements as used in nested regional climate models (RCMs) such as ICON. This limitation prevents their application in practical settings where spatial discretization changes over time or across nested domains, causing degradation in accuracy and physical consistency. Existing neural operators either lack explicit grid topology encoding or scale-awareness, hindering transferability to unseen mesh refinements and resolutions.

APPROACH: We propose MSGT-NO, which integrates (1) a multi-scale graph transformer architecture that processes climate state variables on irregular grids via graph message passing augmented with scale-aware attention layers to capture hierarchical spatial dependencies, and (2) dynamic grid topology embeddings that encode local mesh geometry and refinement levels as learned continuous representations, enabling physical residual regularization and physics-informed constraints during training. This framework will be trained end-to-end on multi-resolution ICON model outputs, leveraging both supervised losses on climate variables and physics-based residual norms to ensure fidelity across variable meshes.

EXPERIMENT: Training and evaluation will use the ICON-EU regional climate dataset with multiple mesh refinements (e.g., 13 km, 7 km, and dynamically refined 3 km grids), supplemented by ERA5 reanalysis data for boundary conditions. Baselines include state-of-the-art neural operators: Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), Graph Neural Operators (GNO), and the winning Multi-Scale Neural Operator from the referenced tournament. Metrics are relative L2 error (<5% target improvement over baselines), physical residual norms (energy and mass conservation errors reduced by >10%), and generalization performance on unseen mesh refinements quantified by out-of-distribution error increases <3%. Experimental protocol involves training on a cluster with 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 72 hours, with 5 random seeds for repeatability and ablation studies on scale-aware attention and topology embeddings.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, MSGT-NO explicitly couples neural operator learning with learned continuous embeddings of dynamic grid topology and refinement levels, enabling physically consistent emulation across heterogeneous, irregular, and temporally varying meshes. This approach goes beyond fixed discretization assumptions in existing neural operators and addresses the transferability gap in regional climate emulators with dynamic mesh refinement, which prior methods cannot robustly handle.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a spatio-temporal deep learning emulator with structured sparse attention mechanisms to efficiently and interpretable model long-term, low-frequency climate trends from global climate model outputs, enabling robust acceleration of multi-decadal climate projections.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate models often rely on dense representations that fail to capture persistent, low-frequency climate variability and long-horizon trends, limiting their effectiveness in emulating slow climate dynamics critical for climate change assessment. Existing deep learning RCM emulators focus primarily on short-term or regional scales and lack mechanisms to produce interpretable, sparse representations that reduce complexity while preserving essential climate signals. There is a clear need for a method that explicitly models spatio-temporal sparsity to improve both interpretability and accuracy for long-term global climate emulation.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel spatio-temporal sparse modeling framework integrating structured sparse attention layers within a transformer-inspired architecture, designed to identify and focus on climatologically relevant sparse regions and time intervals across multi-decadal global climate data. The method leverages domain-informed sparsity priors and adaptive thresholding to maintain only critical low-frequency climate features, reducing computational overhead and enhancing interpretability. This approach will be trained end-to-end on multi-century climate simulations with explicit regularization to enforce sparse spatio-temporal pattern learning.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the CMIP6 historical and future scenario ensemble datasets, the CORDEX regional downscaling dataset, and ERA5 reanalysis for observational validation. Baselines for comparison include the Spatio-Temporal Sparse Modeling method (the tournament winner), the Deep Learning Regional Climate Model Emulators (DL-RCM) from 2023, and the Bayesian U-Net emulator for uncertainty quantification (2025). Metrics include explained variance score (target >5% absolute improvement vs baselines), root mean squared error (RMSE) reduction by at least 10% on long-term trend components, and computational latency reduction of 30% during inference. Experiments will use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of 100k GPU-hours, running 5 random seeds per configuration, and include ablations on sparsity regularization strength and attention window size.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior RCM emulators and dense transformer models, our approach explicitly models persistent spatio-temporal sparsity guided by domain knowledge, enabling superior long-term trend emulation and interpretability while reducing computation. Prior methods either fail to capture low-frequency climate dynamics accurately or produce dense, opaque models unsuitable for scientific analysis, whereas our method directly addresses these limitations with sparse, interpretable attention mechanisms tailored for climate applications.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Latency-Constrained Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to discover ultra-efficient deep learning emulators that enable real-time, on-edge atmospheric dynamics emulation with accuracy within 5% RMSE of state-of-the-art models while halving inference latency on embedded devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current high-fidelity climate and atmospheric emulators achieve strong predictive accuracy but are computationally too heavy for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices, limiting real-time, on-site forecasting applications in operational environments. Existing NAS approaches for climate emulators optimize primarily for accuracy, ignoring stringent latency requirements critical for embedded hardware. This results in models unsuitable for real-time inference on low-power platforms such as Raspberry Pi or NVIDIA Jetson Nano.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-objective NAS framework that simultaneously optimizes deep emulator architectures for minimal inference latency under strict hardware constraints and high-fidelity atmospheric dynamics emulation. The search space includes lightweight convolutional and temporal modules tailored for spatiotemporal climate data. Latency is profiled on representative edge devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4) and integrated as a hard constraint or penalty in the NAS objective. The final selected architectures are trained on multi-year reanalysis datasets and fine-tuned to maintain RMSE within 5% of state-of-the-art emulators. Hardware-aware pruning and quantization techniques will further reduce resource usage without compromising accuracy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on ERA5 reanalysis data, the Climate Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) outputs, and the WeatherBench benchmark dataset. Baselines include the state-of-the-art atmospheric emulators from:  
- Liu et al. (2023) neural emulator for ERA5,  
- Khan et al. (2024) U-Net based emulator,  
- Latency-agnostic NAS emulator by Zhang et al. (2023).  
Metrics: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) within 5% of baseline accuracy, inference latency reduced by ≥50% on Raspberry Pi 4 and NVIDIA Jetson Nano, and model size reduction by ≥40%. Experiments include 5 random seeds per architecture, ablations on latency constraint strength, and comparison of hardware-aware pruning vs no pruning.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work optimizing deep learning climate emulators solely for accuracy or transferability, this method explicitly incorporates hardware latency constraints within NAS to produce models that are provably feasible for real-time deployment on embedded edge devices. Prior NAS studies do not target latency on resource-limited platforms nor produce end-to-end validated atmospheric emulators with demonstrated sub-5% accuracy loss at half the inference time on exemplar hardware.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical multi-scale Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) emulator with cross-scale attention mechanisms and uncertainty quantification to enable scalable, accurate, and physically consistent high-resolution climate simulations on HPC clusters.

BACKGROUND: Current high-resolution climate emulation using PINNs suffers from limited scalability due to monolithic architectures and poor multi-scale coupling, causing physical fidelity loss at extreme resolutions and training bottlenecks on HPC resources. Additionally, existing deep learning emulators lack robust uncertainty quantification and cross-scale information fusion critical for capturing complex climate interactions across spatial and temporal scales.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-scale PINN architecture that decomposes climate physics into scale-specific subnetworks interconnected via learned cross-scale attention modules, enabling dynamic information flow and consistent multi-resolution representation. Training is parallelized using model and data parallelism across distributed HPC clusters (e.g., AWS HPC) to handle CMIP6 high-resolution datasets. An integrated Bayesian uncertainty quantification layer leverages variational inference to provide well-calibrated confidence measures alongside predictions. The emulator explicitly enforces physical constraints via embedded PDE residual losses at each scale.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will use the CMIP6 HighResMIP dataset, the CORDEX regional climate projections, and the ERA5 reanalysis data to benchmark spatial RMSE, temporal correlation coefficients, and calibration metrics for uncertainty (e.g., CRPS). Baselines include Multi-Scale PINNs without attention (as in the tournament winner), U-Net based climate emulators, and deep learning downscaling methods like DeepSD. Metrics target >15% RMSE reduction over baseline PINNs, strong scaling efficiency >85% on AWS HPC, and uncertainty calibration with CRPS improvement >10%. Experiments will run on clusters with at least 256 GPUs, employing 5 random seeds and ablation studies on attention and uncertainty components.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous monolithic or simple multi-scale PINNs, this work uniquely integrates cross-scale attention for dynamic feature fusion and uncertainty quantification within a scalable distributed training framework, enabling physically consistent, fine-grained climate emulation with rigorous error bounds—capabilities absent from all cited prior art.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a distributed, multi-GPU physics-informed neural network (PINN) training framework that enforces scalable, fine-grained physics constraints for high-resolution climate emulation on HPC clusters, achieving both physical accuracy and parallel efficiency beyond current PINN approaches.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators lack scalable enforcement of distributed physics-informed constraints when simulating high-resolution climate phenomena over large spatiotemporal datasets, limiting their deployment on HPC clusters. The key problem is the trade-off between physical accuracy enforced via PINNs and parallel computation efficiency, which restricts practical climate model acceleration at fine spatial scales and long temporal horizons.

APPROACH: We propose a novel distributed PINN architecture that partitions high-resolution climate data spatially and temporally across multiple GPUs/nodes, incorporating a hierarchical physics-informed loss formulation that combines local PDE residual enforcement with global consistency constraints via asynchronous gradient aggregation. Additionally, we develop scalable distributed training algorithms that optimize communication overhead and memory use, enabling simultaneous training on multi-petabyte climate datasets. The model fuses high-dimensional physical equations (e.g., Navier-Stokes, thermodynamics) with deep spatiotemporal convolutional and attention modules specialized for climate dynamics.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark on the ClimateNet dataset, CORDEX regional climate projections, and the CMIP6 high-resolution subset, comparing against baselines including classical PINNs, U-Net climate emulators, and graph neural network models from recent literature. Evaluation metrics will include mean squared error (MSE) for key variables (temperature, precipitation) with target >15% absolute MSE reduction over classical PINNs, physics residual norm reduction by >20%, and training speedup of at least 3x on an HPC cluster of 64 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Experiments will run over 5 random seeds with ablations on physics loss components and communication strategies.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either sacrifices physical constraint enforcement for parallelism or struggles with scale, our approach uniquely integrates hierarchical distributed physics-informed constraints with advanced parallel training across HPC clusters, enabling high-resolution climate emulation that is both physically faithful and computationally efficient at scales not previously demonstrated.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource-adaptive ensemble training and compression framework that explicitly maximizes ensemble diversity via a novel diversity-aware loss and structured ensemble pruning, enabling high-quality uncertainty quantification on mobile devices under strict computational and memory constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current ensemble methods for uncertainty quantification (UQ) deliver state-of-the-art predictive uncertainty but are computationally expensive and memory-heavy, making them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained mobile platforms. Existing work inadequately balances ensemble size, member diversity, and computational overhead, often compromising uncertainty quality or exceeding mobile hardware limits. There is a critical need for methods that maintain or improve uncertainty estimation quality while drastically reducing resource usage.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight ensemble training framework wherein a diversity-aware loss function explicitly encourages complementary error behavior among ensemble members during training. This is combined with structured pruning and quantization techniques applied at the ensemble and individual model levels, guided by a resource-adaptive controller that enforces strict latency, memory, and energy budgets typical of mobile devices (e.g., smartphones with ARM CPUs). Our method adaptively selects ensemble size, member architectures, and pruning ratios to maximize predictive diversity and uncertainty quality within these constraints. The framework supports any base architecture (e.g., MobileNetV3) and incorporates a novel mutual information-based metric to quantify ensemble diversity during training.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K, and the PAMAP2 human activity recognition dataset, a representative real-world mobile health dataset. Baselines include Deep Ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017), MC Dropout (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016), and Efficient Ensembles (Lee et al., 2021). Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and inference latency and memory footprint measured on a Snapdragon 8 Gen 1 mobile processor. Target improvements include >2% absolute reduction in ECE vs. Deep Ensembles at equal or lower latency, >30% reduction in memory footprint vs. Efficient Ensembles, and maintaining NLL within 5% of full ensemble baselines. Experiments run across 5 random seeds with extensive ablation on the loss components and pruning strategies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that loosely addresses diversity or applies generic compression post hoc, our method integrates diversity maximization directly into training with a theoretically grounded loss and couples it with resource-constrained adaptive pruning. This joint optimization ensures the ensemble is both diverse and compact from the start, a capability absent in existing PSC, active subspace, or latent-space UQ methods, which do not explicitly optimize diversity-resource trade-offs for mobile deployment.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Integrate a real-time Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Network (Bayes-PINN) architecture that jointly performs PDE-constrained inference and uncertainty quantification via an embedded low-dimensional active subspace, enabling rapid, calibrated uncertainty estimates for coupled fluid-structure dynamics under 50ms latency.

BACKGROUND: Current methods for uncertainty quantification (UQ) in fluid-structure interaction often decouple PDE solving and Bayesian inference or rely on expensive offline calibration, resulting in latency too high for real-time aerospace applications. This latency impedes immediate uncertainty awareness critical for safe decision-making during extreme flight conditions, such as aeroelastic flutter or turbulence-induced vibrations. Existing SOTA lacks a unified framework that tightly couples physics constraints, Bayesian inference, and dimension reduction for fast yet reliable UQ under stringent latency budgets.

APPROACH: We propose a Bayes-PINN framework embedding a reduced-order active subspace representation of neural network parameters, identified via gradient-based sensitivity analysis on PDE residuals, to drastically lower inference dimensionality. This compressed latent space is integrated with a custom variational Bayesian inference scheme conditioned on fluid-structure PDE constraints, enabling immediate propagation of physics-informed uncertainty estimates. Efficient probabilistic skip connections facilitate uncertainty flow between latent and observable layers, maintaining interpretability of uncertainty in coupled aeroelastic outputs. The entire pipeline is optimized end-to-end with a latency-aware loss function to enforce inference within 50ms on GPU hardware.

EXPERIMENT: Validate on NASA’s Aeroelasticity Prediction Challenge dataset, NASA Turbulence Modeling Challenge dataset, and the SU2 CFD benchmark dataset for fluid-structure problems. Baselines include DeepONet with MC Dropout, Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC), and the original Real-time Bayesian PINNs without active subspace reduction. Evaluate expected calibration error (target <0.05), negative log-likelihood, and inference latency (target <50ms). Conduct ablation over active subspace dimension and skip connection architecture with 5 repeated runs on NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

NOVELTY: This method tightly couples Bayesian inference with PDE constraints and active subspace dimension reduction for real-time UQ, unlike prior Bayes-PINNs that do not exploit parameter subspace compression or probabilistic skip connections for uncertainty flow. Unlike PSC or latent-space UQ alone, our approach achieves interpretable, calibrated uncertainty under strict latency constraints in complex fluid-structure systems, a capability existing methods cannot demonstrate.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate causally-structured Bayesian deep neural networks with active subspace identification and Bhattacharyya coefficient-based uncertainty calibration to disentangle confounders and produce robust, interpretable, and ethically constrained uncertainty estimates in clinical diagnostic predictions under data scarcity.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods in deep neural networks (DNNs) fail to robustly separate causal effects from spurious correlations in heterogeneous clinical datasets, leading to unreliable uncertainty estimates that undermine clinical trust. Existing approaches neglect explicit causal and domain constraints, and do not adequately address uncertainty calibration across subpopulations or the influence of latent confounders, particularly under limited data availability. This results in biased or overconfident predictions that can exacerbate health disparities.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework that embeds expert-annotated causal graphs and domain-specific ethical constraints into a Bayesian neural network architecture, leveraging active subspace learning to identify and focus uncertainty quantification on parameter directions most relevant to causal effects. To further enhance uncertainty calibration, we incorporate a Bhattacharyya coefficient-based metric to measure the overlap between output distributions, improving inter-class confusion detection. This combined method explicitly disentangles confounders, enforces causal interpretability, and adapts to heterogeneous patient subgroups by correcting biased uncertainty estimates arising from data scarcity.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on the MIMIC-III, eICU, and PhysioNet Sepsis datasets, comparing against baselines: Monte Carlo Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and Probabilistic Skip Connections. Key metrics include Expected Calibration Error (target <0.05), Causal Disentanglement Score (quantified via average treatment effect recovery with >10% improvement over baselines), and Bhattacharyya-based inter-class confusion reduction (>15% decrease). Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with five random seeds and include ablation studies isolating causal graph integration, active subspace learning, and Bhattacharyya calibration components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as Probabilistic Skip Connections and latent space inference methods, this approach explicitly incorporates causal domain knowledge and ethical constraints within a Bayesian framework combined with active subspace identification and Bhattacharyya-based uncertainty calibration. This allows principled disentangling of causal effects from confounders under data scarcity and heterogeneity, yielding robust, interpretable, and unbiased uncertainty estimates that prior methods cannot achieve.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
A meta-learning framework for calibrated uncertainty quantification that dynamically adapts to individual users’ non-stationary data streams on resource-constrained edge devices in personalized health monitoring, optimizing simultaneously for low power, limited memory, and real-time operation.

BACKGROUND:  
Personalized health monitoring on edge devices faces the open problem of reliably quantifying uncertainty under strict hardware constraints and continuously evolving user-specific data distributions. Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods either require expensive computations or fail to maintain calibration when adapting to new users or changing physiological states, especially in real-time and low-resource settings. There is a lack of approaches that jointly optimize for rapid adaptation, uncertainty calibration, and system efficiency on-device.

APPROACH:  
We propose a meta-learning algorithm that trains a compact uncertainty prediction module conditioned on lightweight feature embeddings extracted by a frozen backbone network. During deployment, it quickly adapts uncertainty estimates per user by fine-tuning only the lightweight module with few-shot on-device updates, leveraging a calibration-aware loss that penalizes miscalibration under distribution shifts. The method exploits neural architecture search within strict resource budgets to identify the minimal model configuration meeting latency and memory constraints on target hardware (e.g., ARM Cortex-M or Qualcomm Snapdragon). The framework incorporates continual learning strategies to handle non-stationary streams without catastrophic forgetting, maintaining uncertainty calibration throughout.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on three real-world personalized health datasets with temporal and user-specific shifts: PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, MIMIC-III physiological time series, and the WESAD multimodal stress dataset. Baselines will include MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and the Meta-UQ approach from the 2023 meta-learning literature for edge devices. Metrics will measure uncertainty calibration (Expected Calibration Error, Negative Log Likelihood), adaptation speed (few-shot adaptation epochs and latency), and resource usage (peak memory footprint, power consumption on representative hardware). The goal is >15% reduction in calibration error versus baselines post-adaptation, <100ms per inference latency on-device, and <50MB peak memory usage. Experiments will run with 5 random seeds, and ablation studies will isolate the impact of meta-learning, calibration losses, and architecture search.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior UQ methods that lack simultaneous optimization for strict on-device resource constraints, rapid user-specific adaptation, and calibration under non-stationarity, this work explicitly integrates meta-learning with calibration-aware objectives and neural architecture search for edge deployment in personalized health. It uniquely addresses the triad of trustworthy uncertainty, continuous adaptation, and hardware efficiency in real-time health monitoring settings.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Normalizing Flow framework that explicitly disentangles aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties by learning separate invertible latent subspaces for noise and model uncertainty, enabling superior out-of-distribution detection and uncertainty calibration on complex image benchmarks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative uncertainty methods based on normalizing flows conflate aleatoric (data-inherent) and epistemic (model) uncertainties within a single likelihood or predictive variance estimate, leading to poor out-of-distribution (OOD) detection and unreliable confidence intervals. This entanglement limits robustness and interpretability, especially in safety-critical domains where distinguishing noise-driven from knowledge-driven uncertainty is essential. Existing techniques like probabilistic skip connections and latent-space inference capture uncertainty but do not perform explicit uncertainty component decomposition in normalizing flows.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel normalizing flow architecture with dual latent spaces: one dedicated to modeling aleatoric uncertainty (noise subspace) and another modeling epistemic uncertainty (model subspace). By imposing structured invertible transformations and orthogonality constraints between these subspaces, the flow disentangles the two uncertainty types during likelihood estimation. The model jointly optimizes data manifold density and predictive variance decomposition, enabling separate quantification of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. This dual-subspace decomposition facilitates improved OOD detection by isolating model uncertainty signals and provides calibrated uncertainty intervals by separately controlling noise-induced variance.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on CIFAR-10 vs CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet-1K OOD detection tasks; compare against Deep Ensemble, MC Dropout, Bayesian Neural Networks, and the baseline normalizing flow-based method from the tournament winner paper. Metrics will include OOD detection AUROC (>5% absolute improvement expected over tournament winner), Expected Calibration Error (ECE) reduction by ≥3%, and negative log-likelihood (NLL). Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per condition, including ablations on subspace dimensionality and orthogonality constraints.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this method explicitly decomposes aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties within a normalizing flow via structurally disentangled latent spaces, rather than conflating them in a single likelihood or variance estimate. This leads to improved interpretability and robustness in uncertainty quantification and provides clearer uncertainty signals for OOD detection beyond what probabilistic skip connections, latent-space inference, or Bhattacharyya coefficient overlap techniques can offer.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical, memory-adaptive contrastive foundation model architecture with dynamic embedding compression to enable real-time, multimodal integration of single-cell multi-omic data on ultra-low-memory portable sequencing devices while maintaining cross-modal biological fidelity.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models for integrating multi-omic single-cell datasets require large memory footprints that exceed the RAM constraints of edge sequencing devices such as portable ARM-based sequencers. This leads to crashes or severe slowdowns, preventing deployment in low-resource clinical environments. Existing lightweight contrastive models reduce memory usage but sacrifice cross-modal consistency or cannot dynamically adapt compression based on input complexity, limiting their applicability to diverse biological contexts and real-time workloads.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel hierarchical contrastive model architecture featuring multi-level parameter sharing across genomic, transcriptomic, and imaging modalities combined with an adaptive embedding compression mechanism that dynamically adjusts embedding dimensionality and quantization granularity conditioned on input complexity and modality. This system employs a memory-adaptive module that monitors runtime RAM usage and modulates model components to maintain operation below 500MB on ARM-based edge devices. Training leverages a curriculum contrastive loss aligning multi-omic signals at multiple abstraction levels, ensuring robust, cross-modal biological representation without degrading alignment quality.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the 10x Genomics Multiome dataset, Human Cell Atlas single-cell multi-omic data, and the Allen Brain Map multimodal single-cell datasets. Baselines include Memory-efficient Contrastive Models for Portable Multi-omic Sequencers (the tournament winner), CLOT (Contrastive Learning of Single-Cell Omics), and scMoMaT (Single-cell Multi-omics Multi-modal Alignment Transformer). Metrics: achieve ≥3% absolute improvement in alignment F1 score over the tournament winner, maintain peak RAM usage under 500MB on ARM Cortex-A72 hardware, and reduce inference latency by ≥25%. Conduct ablation studies on adaptive compression, hierarchical parameter sharing, and curriculum loss efficacy over 5 random seeds with a fixed training compute budget of 48 GPU hours on NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies static embedding compression and uniform parameter sharing unable to dynamically optimize memory-accuracy trade-offs, our hierarchical architecture with runtime adaptive embedding compression uniquely enables real-time, robust multi-omic alignment within strict memory budgets on portable devices, preserving cross-modal biological consistency and extending applicability to diverse, dynamic single-cell data without manual model reconfiguration.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a causal representation learning framework that explicitly models and adjusts for genetic population stratification and environmental confounders in foundation models trained on large-scale, multi-ancestry genomic and single-cell datasets to enable robust causal inference of disease mechanisms.

BACKGROUND: Current foundation models in genomics and single-cell biology inadequately handle confounding factors such as population stratification and environmental heterogeneity when analyzing diverse, population-scale datasets. This leads to biased or spurious associations that obscure true causal biological drivers, limiting the translational impact of these models in personalized medicine and disease etiology studies. There is a critical need for models that integrate genetic diversity and environmental covariates while explicitly controlling for confounders to enable valid causal inference at population scale.

APPROACH: We propose a novel causal representation learning framework that incorporates population structure inference (e.g., via principal component analysis or variational autoencoders trained on genotype data) and environmental covariate embeddings directly into the latent space of a multi-modal foundation model jointly trained on genomics and single-cell transcriptomics data. The model leverages invariant risk minimization and disentangled representation learning to isolate causal signals from confounders, supported by explicit adjustment layers that remove stratification effects in downstream prediction and causal effect estimation tasks. This framework enables robust transfer across ancestries and environmental contexts while preserving biological interpretability.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on benchmark multi-ancestry genomic datasets such as the UK Biobank Phase 3, the PAGE Study (Population Architecture using Genomics and Epidemiology), and the Human Cell Atlas single-cell RNA-seq datasets linked to donor metadata including ancestry and environmental exposures. Baselines include DeepVariant, BERT-based genomic language models (DNABERT), scBERT (single-cell transformer), and recent causal inference models like CausalVAE and DeepIV. Metrics will assess causal effect estimation accuracy (average treatment effect error <10%), confounding bias reduction (measured by stratification test statistics reduction >25%), and predictive performance (AUROC improvement >3% over baselines on disease phenotype classification). Experimental protocol involves training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 3 random seeds, ablation studies removing confounder adjustment layers, and cross-ancestry generalization tests.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing single-cell and genomic foundation models that treat genetic diversity as covariates or ignore confounding, this approach explicitly integrates causal representation learning with population stratification control mechanisms within a foundation model framework. It uniquely enables valid causal inference on large-scale, multi-ancestry genomic and single-cell data, a capability lacking in prior scFMs and genomic language models that focus on association rather than causation.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Self-Supervised Foundation Model for Rare Disease Single-Cell Genomics using Biologically-Informed Multimodal Masking that jointly models nucleotide sequences, epigenetic states, and single-cell transcriptomic profiles, prioritizing functionally critical regions to improve phenotype prediction and rare variant interpretation under extreme data scarcity.

BACKGROUND:  
Current self-supervised foundation models in genomics and single-cell biology largely rely on random masking strategies that do not account for biological relevance, which dilutes scarce informative signals in rare disease datasets, limiting downstream phenotype and variant effect prediction accuracy. Moreover, existing models either focus on bulk genomic or single-modality single-cell data, failing to integrate multimodal single-cell signals (genomic, epigenomic, transcriptomic) that are crucial for understanding complex rare disease mechanisms at cellular resolution. There is a pressing need for models that integrate multimodal single-cell data with biologically-informed masking to enhance representation learning and rare disease genotype-to-phenotype modeling.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multimodal masked modeling architecture that jointly learns representations from nucleotide sequences, chromatin accessibility (e.g., ATAC-seq), and single-cell RNA-seq data by applying biologically-informed masking strategies prioritizing regulatory elements, conserved regions, and cell-type-specific features. The masking scheme leverages curated biological priors such as ENCODE regulatory annotations, evolutionary conservation scores (PhastCons), and single-cell metadata-driven cell-type markers to selectively mask informative regions rather than random masking. The model architecture extends transformer-based masked language modeling to handle multimodal inputs via cross-attention layers that integrate modalities, enabling a unified embedding space representing genomic and single-cell functional states. Fine-tuning on rare disease cohorts enhances phenotype prediction, variant pathogenicity scoring, and cell-state deconvolution.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate on multiple rare disease genomics and single-cell datasets:  
- Undiagnosed Diseases Network (UDN) whole-genome and single-cell RNA-seq datasets  
- Human Cell Atlas (HCA) multiomic single-cell datasets for diverse tissues  
- Nephrobase Cell+ single-cell kidney disease dataset  
Baselines: BERT-base genomic masked modeling, Enformer, scBERT, and Nephrobase Cell+ without biologically-informed masking  
Metrics: >3% absolute F1 improvement in rare disease phenotype classification, >5% improvement in variant pathogenicity AUC on ClinVar rare variants, reduction of perplexity by 10% on masked prediction tasks.  
Protocol: Training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for ~5 days, experiments repeated over 3 seeds, ablation studies comparing random vs biologically-informed masking, unimodal vs multimodal inputs, and different masking heuristics.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior masked modeling frameworks that use random or single-modality masking, this method uniquely integrates biologically-informed masking across multimodal single-cell data, explicitly optimizing for representation fidelity in rare disease contexts with limited labeled data. This cross-modal biologically-prioritized masking approach enables superior phenotype and variant effect prediction under extreme data scarcity, a capability current single-cell foundation models and genomics language models lack.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a highly efficient multimodal contrastive single-cell foundation model architecture optimized for real-time inference on edge devices, enabling immediate multimodal (transcriptomic, proteomic, and imaging) single-cell diagnostics with minimal latency and resource usage.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models deliver rich multimodal representations but require substantial computational resources and incur high inference latency, restricting their deployment to powerful cloud or laboratory hardware. This bottleneck prevents real-time, point-of-care single-cell analysis critical for rapid clinical decision-making in resource-constrained environments like bedside diagnostics or portable devices. There is a pressing need for models that preserve multimodal representation quality while drastically reducing inference complexity to enable edge deployment.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight, modular multimodal contrastive learning architecture combining efficient transformer variants (e.g., Linformer or Performer) and neural network pruning with knowledge distillation from large pre-trained scFMs like Nephrobase Cell+ and the latest multimodal scFMs. A hardware-aware neural architecture search (NAS) will optimize model components for edge device constraints (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, NVIDIA Jetson). The model integrates transcriptomic, proteomic, and cellular imaging data using shared latent embeddings. Real-time quantization and dynamic batching techniques further reduce latency while preserving diagnostic accuracy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Benchmark on multimodal single-cell datasets including Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell multiome ATAC+RNA, 10x Genomics CITE-seq datasets, and the Nephrobase Cell+ kidney single-cell dataset. Baselines include Nephrobase Cell+, Multimodal Contrastive Learning (MCL) scFM, and efficient transformer models like Perceiver IO. Metrics: latency reduction target >50% versus Nephrobase Cell+ on NVIDIA Jetson Nano; classification F1 score increase or within 2% of baseline on cell-type identification; memory footprint under 500MB; energy consumption reduction >40%. Experiments run on edge hardware with 5 random seeds; ablations on pruning ratio, quantization precision, and NAS search spaces.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which focuses on accuracy or cross-species robustness without hardware constraints, this approach explicitly co-designs model architecture and training for edge deployment, delivering a practical, real-time multimodal single-cell diagnostic tool. It is the first to combine multimodal contrastive learning with hardware-aware NAS and aggressive model compression for single-cell foundation models, enabling clinical-grade inference on portable devices unseen in prior literature.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable multimodal foundation model architecture trained with robust cross-modal contrastive learning on globally diverse, large-scale population genomics and single-cell multi-omics datasets to generate expressive embeddings that generalize across heterogeneous cohorts and modalities.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology lack scalability and robustness when applied across heterogeneous cohorts with complex multimodal data (e.g., genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics). Existing models struggle to capture subtle biological variation across global populations and fail to integrate multiple data modalities effectively at scale. This limitation hampers population genomics efforts essential for precision medicine and evolutionary biology insights.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel scalable multimodal architecture that jointly embeds genomic sequences, single-cell transcriptomes, and epigenomic profiles using a unified transformer-based encoder with modality-specific adapters. Training leverages a robust cross-modal contrastive learning objective designed to emphasize fine-grained biological variation while mitigating batch and cohort effects. A curriculum learning strategy progressively incorporates increasingly diverse global datasets to improve generalization. We introduce scalable data pipelines integrating large datasets such as gnomAD, 1000 Genomes Project, and the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell multi-omics data, enabling model pretraining over billions of paired data points.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark against baselines including Enformer, scBERT, and Nephrobase Cell+ on datasets: 1000 Genomes Project (whole genomes), gnomAD v3.1 (population variation), and Human Cell Atlas multi-omics data (paired scRNA-seq and ATAC-seq). Evaluation metrics include cross-modal retrieval accuracy (target >5% absolute improvement over Nephrobase Cell+), population stratification clustering metrics (Adjusted Rand Index improvements >0.1), and downstream prediction tasks such as variant effect prediction (ROC-AUC >0.85) and cell type classification accuracy (>90%). Experiments will run on clusters of 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, with five random seeds per experiment and ablations on contrastive loss variants and curriculum training schedules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior models which are limited to either single modalities or small, homogeneous cohorts, this method integrates large-scale, globally diverse population genomics with paired single-cell multi-omics under a unified, scalable training framework. The robust cross-modal contrastive objective and curriculum learning enable generalization across data modalities and populations, directly addressing the scalability and heterogeneity gaps highlighted but unaddressed by existing single-cell foundation models and population genomic frameworks.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical bandwidth-limited emergent communication framework that learns multi-level semantic compression via concept embeddings combined with adaptive discrete codebooks, enabling dynamic trade-offs between communication cost and task-specific informativeness in multi-agent reinforcement learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods like "Bandwidth-Limited Semantic Compression via Learned Concept Embeddings" learn fixed-size continuous embeddings to compress messages for multi-agent communication but do not dynamically adapt message granularity or discretize communication to further reduce bandwidth while preserving semantics. This limits efficiency in scenarios with highly variable communication constraints or heterogeneous agent roles. Thus, the key open problem is how to jointly learn hierarchical, discrete semantic representations that can flexibly scale bandwidth usage without sacrificing task performance in multi-agent environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel hierarchical compression architecture that trains agents to encode messages into multi-level semantic embeddings: a coarse discrete codebook layer capturing high-level concepts and a finer continuous embedding layer refining semantics as needed. The discrete codebook is learned via vector quantization with a differentiable Gumbel-softmax relaxation to enable end-to-end training. An adaptive bandwidth controller module dynamically selects the message level based on task context and communication constraints, enabling agents to send either compact discrete codes or richer continuous refinements when bandwidth permits. This joint learning optimizes semantic informativeness subject to strict, context-dependent message size constraints, leveraging a combined RL and mutual information objective.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three multi-agent benchmarks—Google Research Football, StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC), and Hanabi—comparing against baselines including the original Bandwidth-Limited Semantic Compression method, the Neuro-spike Communication framework (2025), and Positive Signalling/Listening biases (2019). Metrics include >5% absolute improvement in task reward efficiency per communicated bit, reduction of average transmitted bits by >40%, and communication latency measured by message size and transmission steps. Training will be conducted on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for up to 5 million environment steps per task, with 5 random seeds per experiment. Ablations will test the impact of discrete codebooks, the adaptive bandwidth controller, and joint mutual information objectives.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, our method enables dynamic, hierarchical semantic compression combining discrete and continuous representations, allowing agents to flexibly allocate communication bandwidth based on task demands—a capability neither fixed-size embedding compression nor neuro-spike binary signals achieve. This adaptability leads to substantial communication efficiency gains without task performance loss, concretely addressing bandwidth variability and semantic richness trade-offs missing in existing frameworks.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a neuro-inspired, adaptive spike coding framework that dynamically balances information fidelity and energy efficiency in multi-agent emergent communication for ultra-low-power edge IoT networks with intermittent connectivity.

BACKGROUND: Current neuro-spike communication frameworks for multi-agent systems either incur excessive energy costs or sacrifice communication fidelity, limiting their real-world applicability on ultra-low-power edge IoT devices. The open problem is enabling robust emergent communication protocols that can adaptively compress and encode messages as sparse spike trains while maintaining task-critical information throughput under severe energy and hardware constraints, which existing methods do not address.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive spike encoding mechanism combining biologically-inspired event-driven encoding with reinforcement learning-driven message compression that dynamically regulates spike timing, amplitude, and sequence complexity based on environmental context and agent cooperation needs. This system integrates a hierarchical neuro-spike encoder-decoder with an energy-aware communication scheduler to optimize the tradeoff between communication accuracy and power consumption. Multi-agent coordination is enhanced through emergent protocols learned end-to-end, leveraging spike-timing dependent plasticity-inspired updates to refine communication over episodic training.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE), the SMART IoT dataset for edge device communication patterns, and the OpenAI Multi-Agent Traffic Control benchmark; compare to baselines including standard neuro-spike coding (A Note on Emergent Behavior 2025), Biases for Emergent Communication (2019), and Firefly-inspired vocabulary generator (2022). Metrics include >3% absolute improvement in task success rate, >25% reduction in communication energy consumption measured in simulated Joules, and >15% decrease in average message latency. Use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 72-hour training budget per seed, 5 random seeds per experiment, with ablation studies on scheduler components and spike encoding parameters.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior neuro-spike methods which use fixed or simplistic spike encodings, this work proposes an adaptive, context-aware encoding and scheduling framework that jointly optimizes energy and fidelity in emergent multi-agent communication on constrained hardware. This system explicitly incorporates dynamic resource constraints and intermittent connectivity into the encoding policy, enabling scalable, robust coordination beyond existing fixed-bit or single-bit spike communication schemes.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
A robustness-driven co-evolutionary framework that simultaneously optimizes multi-agent communication protocols and adaptive behaviors under diverse real-world uncertainties, explicitly incorporating noise, partial observability, and channel failures into the evolutionary fitness to produce resilient, interpretable communication systems validated on challenging multi-agent benchmarks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems exhibit brittleness when exposed to realistic communication constraints such as noise, partial observability, and dynamic environmental changes. Existing gradient-based and evolutionary approaches optimize performance under idealized conditions but fail to maintain efficacy and reliability across noisy or degraded channels, limiting their practical deployment in domains like robotics and distributed sensing. The open problem is to develop communication protocols and agent behaviors that maintain cooperative task success and communication interpretability despite these uncertainties.

APPROACH:  
We propose a Robustness-Driven Evolutionary Protocol and Behavior Co-Optimization (RDE-PBCO) framework that employs multi-objective evolutionary algorithms to jointly evolve communication vocabularies and agent policies with explicit robustness constraints. The fitness function integrates (1) task success rate under varying levels and types of channel noise (Gaussian, burst errors, dropout), (2) variance in performance to encourage stability across noisy conditions, and (3) interpretability regularizers based on mutual information and signaling efficiency. Agents are evolved in simulated multi-agent environments with introduced communication degradations and partial observability to drive the emergence of adaptive, fault-tolerant communication behaviors. Robustness metrics such as success rate variance under noise and channel failure tolerance guide the selection of robust protocols.

EXPERIMENT:  
We validate RDE-PBCO on the following benchmark multi-agent tasks: the predator-prey environment from the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE), the Google Research Football environment (GRF) in multi-agent mode, and the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC). Baselines include: the evolutionary protocol from “Robustness-Driven Evolutionary Protocol and Behavior Co-Optimization” (prior winner), bias-augmented emergent communication from “Biases for Emergent Communication in Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning” (2019), and the neuro-spike communication framework from “A Note on Emergent Behavior in Multi-agent Systems Enabled by Neuro-spike Communication” (2025). Metrics are task success rate (>5% absolute improvement over baselines under noise), robustness measured by performance variance (reduce variance by ≥30%), and communication efficiency measured by bits-per-message (reduce by ≥15% without loss of success rate). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 seeds per condition; ablations analyze noise types, partial observability degrees, and interpretability regularizer impact.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing inductive bias or neuro-spike communication methods that do not explicitly optimize for robustness under realistic noisy and partially observable channels, RDE-PBCO uniquely integrates robustness metrics directly into the evolutionary fitness, enabling the co-evolution of both communication and behavior that sustain task performance and interpretability despite communication failures. This approach produces emergent protocols that are demonstrably more reliable and stable in challenging environments where prior methods fail.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:
